comments.text: Fresh compliance PASS after finding and fixing the residual replace_one builder: read/count/delete append every requested version after any selector, version-only never becomes 1=1, exact bind order is preserved, and replace fails closed when metadata has no version column. Key/filter/keys/version-only/native-invalid and replace guards cover all SQL builders. No blocker.

title: [DRVQA25][P1][security] Make capture redaction stateful and fail closed for split or long secrets
description:
Finding

The ORACLEDB_CAPTURE scrubber scans each recorded transport frame independently and redacts only a fixed 512-byte window after a recognized auth marker. Long AUTH_TOKEN/password values retain their suffix, while arbitrary partial reads/writes can split marker and value across frames so most or all secret bytes survive. The final gate scans marker labels that redaction already erased, so it cannot detect the orphaned secret suffix.

Evidence

transport.rs uses REDACT_WINDOW=512, find_marker and scrub_frame per frame. Recording boundaries follow arbitrary partial transport reads/writes. Token auth accepts arbitrary-length strings. Existing tests cover only short same-frame values and a complete marker outside the window.

Impact

A cassette documented as secret-free and safe to share can persist OAuth/IAM tokens or passwords to disk and source control.

False-positive and duplicate checks

A single same-frame token over roughly 500 bytes deterministically leaks, independent of socket chunking; split frames are also reachable. Full tracker search found no exact issue.

Proposed fix

Statefully parse/reassemble capture records and redact exact length-delimited auth values, or conservatively omit the entire authentication phase. Any ambiguous/truncated auth-shaped sequence must refuse artifact creation. Gate the unsanitized logical stream as well as sanitized output; never rely only on erased labels.

Guard tests

Unique 2 KiB and 64 KiB tokens/passwords leave no suffix; exercise every split point across marker/length/value and partial reads/writes; mixed fields redact all; ambiguity fails closed before writing; ordinary non-secret cassettes remain byte-stable.
notes: Verified true credential-capture vulnerability. Pre-fix regressions proved a 2 KiB/64 KiB token suffix leaked beyond the fixed 512-byte window, a marker/value split across recorder frames leaked the value, and a marker split across frames evaded redaction. Root cause was per-frame scanning plus fixed-window erasure followed by a gate that could no longer recognize orphaned suffixes. Fixed by scanning contiguous same-direction transport runs, case-insensitively detecting markers across arbitrary split points, and fail-closed redacting marker-to-run-end when marker begins in the auth window. Opposite-direction response data remains intact. Guard tests cover 64 KiB tokens, 4 KiB passwords, every split point across AUTH_TOKEN marker/value, split markers, beyond-window refusal, direction boundaries, and persisted/replay behavior. Final cassette lib: 276 passed, 5 ignored; cassette clippy -D warnings and fmt/diff checks pass. UBS findings triaged as test-only panic/equality heuristics. Independent fresh-eyes review PASS; token length/chunking and persisted-leak reachability independently confirmed.
close_reason: Verified capture leak, fixed stateful fail-closed redaction, regression-tested, and independently reviewed.

title: [DRVQA25][P1][cursor] Release OwnedRowStream cursor state after continuation-fetch errors
description:
Finding

When an OwnedRowStream continuation fetch returns an error, poll_next restores the Connection but sets cursor_id to zero without releasing or closing the previous cursor. Drop and into_connection then skip cleanup. Connection registries retain the cursor as in use, copied cursors are never queued for close, and the cached SQL cannot reuse the retained cursor.

Evidence

row_stream.rs fetch-error branch zeroes cursor_id. release_cursor_if_open and Drop act only on nonzero ids. lib.rs release_cursor clears in_use_cursors and copied_cursors; statement_cache_get refuses in-use ids and repeated same-SQL execution creates copies. The existing live midstream error test closes the recovered connection immediately and cannot observe accumulation.

Impact

Repeated continuation errors on a reusable connection accumulate client registry entries and server cursors, eventually causing memory growth, cache churn, or ORA-01000.

False-positive and duplicate checks

A server error may invalidate the cursor, but client lifecycle state still must be cleared. Borrowing Rows retains the id for cleanup. Full tracker search found no exact issue.

Proposed fix

Retain the previous id and explicitly close-and-remove it from all client registries before zeroing. Prefer close_cursor over cache release when cursor validity after the error is uncertain; pre-wire cancellation may use normal release only if proven safe.

Guard tests

Repeated midstream server errors and pre-wire cancellation keep in-use/copied/close queues bounded; same SQL re-executes without copy accumulation; server open-cursor count stays bounded in live coverage; the stream emits exactly one error, terminates, and returns a reusable connection.
notes: Verified true. Pre-fix continuation fetch errors returned the connection but poll_next zeroed the cursor id without releasing or closing it, so Drop/into_connection skipped cleanup and cached/in-use/copied/metadata/LOB registries retained stale state. The fix centralizes fail-closed close_cursor_on_error, evicts every cache entry by cursor id, clears all registries, and deduplicates close piggybacks. Owned and borrowing continuation, scroll, materialized DEFINE/FETCH, Arrow cold-define, collect, and refetch paths now retire uncertain cursors; proven BeforeStart cancellation alone releases the valid cached cursor; InFlight timeout performs BREAK/reset/drain recovery before retirement. Deterministic peer-EOF, copied/idempotent, BeforeStart, and true InFlight regressions pass. Fresh independent review PASS: lib 256/256, focused deadline tests, fmt, production clippy. Two explicit live Oracle tests pass for midstream ORA-01476 then connection reuse and materialized-CLOB DEFINE/FETCH failure then identical-SQL reuse. UBS staged shadow check/clippy/test-no-run/audit/deny passed; reported findings were existing tests/heuristics and no introduced unsafe or security issue.
close_reason: Confirmed cursor leak/cache poisoning after fetch errors; all affected cursor paths now retire uncertain cursors with deterministic and live Oracle guards.

title: [DRVQA25][P1][deadline] Do not send BREAK when a deadline expired before polling
description:
Finding

QueryDeadline::run returns timeout immediately when its deadline is already expired, before polling the operation future. Callers treat that result identically to an in-flight timeout and unconditionally run BREAK plus drain recovery on a healthy idle session even though no request bytes could have been sent.

Evidence

request.rs checks remaining time before awaiting the future and returns Err immediately. Query, execute and owning-stream callers map every Err to recover_from_call_timeout; that sends the Oracle BREAK packet and drains a cancellation response.

Impact

A zero timeout or already-expired ambient Cx can turn an immediate local timeout into recovery latency, protocol desynchronization, or ConnectionClosed, and writes an unsolicited BREAK to an idle server.

False-positive and duplicate checks

BREAK is required after the future was polled and may have written bytes. The pre-expired branch proves this future was never polled. Full tracker search found no exact issue.

Proposed fix

Return a typed deadline outcome distinguishing ExpiredBeforePoll from ElapsedInFlight. Return the user timeout/cancellation directly for the former; invoke BREAK/drain only for the latter. Keep cancellation-on-drop behavior separate.

Guard tests

Zero timeout and expired ambient deadline return immediately with zero transport writes and a reusable Ready connection. A deliberately stalled in-flight request still emits exactly one BREAK and drains or marks Dead according to existing policy.
notes: VERIFIED TRUE BUG. Pre-fix loopback observed an unsolicited TNS BREAK as the first packet for a zero or already-expired deadline, even though the database future was never polled. The fix introduces typed BeforeStart versus InFlight expiry, tracks the actual first poll without making futures non-Send, and invokes BREAK/drain only after a database future was polled. All QueryDeadline callers plus every recovery-bearing legacy timeout wrapper (ping, execute variants, and LOB read/write/trim/free) now use the typed path. BeforeStart preserves structured cancellation: User yields Cancelled on a reusable Ready connection; Shutdown yields ConnectionClosed and marks Dead; both emit zero bytes. Cursor-bearing pre-start paths release their cursor. Guards cover zero explicit timeout, expired ambient deadline, timeout-wrapper-wins-before-inner-poll race, in-flight classification, Send compilation, zero BREAK plus same-socket COMMIT reuse, and zero-wire User/Shutdown semantics. Full oracledb library 254/254; clippy -D warnings, workspace fmt, diff check, UBS build/check/audit/deny pass. UBS source heuristics were pre-existing/test-only or false positives. Independent fresh-eyes review found and drove fixes for timeout-wrapper poll races, Send preservation, structured cancel disposition, flaky timing, and legacy-wrapper coverage, then returned PASS.
close_reason: Verified unsolicited idle BREAK, fixed actual-first-poll timeout classification across all recovery-bearing APIs, preserved cancellation semantics, regression-tested, and independently reviewed.

title: [DRVQA25][P1][LOB] Advance LobReader by actual amount and adopt returned locator
description:
Finding

LobReader advances its 1-based position by the requested amount and discards the locator returned from each LOB read. The protocol response deliberately carries actual amount and an updated locator; short reads are valid because read_lob promises up to the requested amount.

Evidence

lob_stream.rs computes want, calls read_lob, then pos += want and extracts only data. LobReadResult contains data, locator and amount; the TTC parser populates amount and replacement locator. The bundled python-oracledb reference updates both source locator and amount, and LobWriter already adopts replacement locators.

Impact

When Oracle returns fewer units than requested, subsequent reads skip LOB units and silently corrupt/truncate BLOB or CLOB output. Locator replacement is lost, so later reads may use stale locator state.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Fixed temp-LOB tests commonly return exactly want and mask the defect. CLOB byte length cannot substitute for returned character-unit amount. Full tracker search found no exact issue.

Proposed fix

Adopt a nonempty returned locator and advance with checked arithmetic by validated result.amount. Reject zero progress before declared EOF and amounts greater than want as protocol errors. Keep units distinct from data byte length.

Guard tests

Script want=8 returning amount=3 and locator A to B; next read must use offset 4 and B. Cover short BLOB, multibyte CLOB, locator replacement, zero progress, oversized amount/overflow, full chunks, and byte-identical read_to_end.
notes: Verified true LOB streaming correctness bug against pinned python-oracledb v4.0.1 semantics and deterministic TTC behavior. Pre-fix 6 regressions failed while only the ordinary full-chunk case passed: short reads advanced by requested units, replacement locators were discarded, and zero/oversized/overflow outcomes could corrupt state. Fixed LobReader to validate returned progress, require payload data for positive progress, adopt nonempty replacement locators, advance by checked result.amount in protocol units, and mutate only after validation. Guards cover short BLOB, multibyte/astral CLOB units, locator rotation, zero/oversized/missing-data/overflow failures, full chunks, and byte-identical two-round-trip read_to_end whose second request uses locator B offset 4 amount 8 (old code used locator A offset 9 amount 3). Focused 8/8, tracing-focused 7/7 before the additional invariant, full lib 250/250 before root strengthening, live Oracle BLOB+CLOB 2/2, clippy/fmt/diff green. UBS test-only heuristics triaged. Independent fresh-eyes review PASS; naturally occurring live short-read/locator rotation remains deterministically proven by loopback TTC.
close_reason: Verified actual-progress/locator corruption, fixed fail-closed LOB state transitions, regression-tested including live Oracle, and independently reviewed.

title: [DRVQA25][P3][docs] Reconcile 0.8.1/0.8.2 release history and K10 status
description:
Finding

CHANGELOG.md stops at 0.7.3 despite the workspace and shipped release being 0.8.2. docs/design/k10-row-stream.md still says the owned row stream is a tracked follow-up not in 0.8.2 and describes it in future tense, although row_stream.rs and the public API shipped.

Impact

Users and maintainers cannot recover what 0.8.1/0.8.2 delivered, and the design document directly contradicts the current public surface.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Current Cargo versions and source export OwnedRowStream at 0.8.2. Full tracker search found no exact release-history cleanup issue.

Proposed fix

Reconstruct 0.8.0 through 0.8.2 entries from tags/commits/release artifacts and mark the K10 document implemented with accurate final API and residual risks. Avoid invented dates or claims; cite bead/commit evidence.

Guard tests

Release-doc checks assert current crate version appears in CHANGELOG and the K10 status cannot say not in the current version; links and examples compile or are validated.
notes: Verified documentation drift and reconstructed the 0.7.4 through 0.8.2 release spine from git tags, GitHub Releases, tag ranges, source, API/provenance ledgers, and representative commits. Fresh review caught and corrected three provenance errors: v0.8.2 used per-page stream deadlines, did not retire failed cursors, and its five-lane matrix did not execute the K10 live suite. The final design record separates the shipped tag from post-release fixes 0708550, b16d42d, and 02eef5e; manual Free23/XE21 K10 evidence is stated separately from the broader matrix. CHANGELOG Unreleased records all five post-tag fixes and distinguishes lightweight from annotated tag provenance by saying git tags. Release preflight now fails closed on missing/unreadable docs, requires an anchored exact version heading, and requires a positive workspace-version K10 status. Independent rereview PASS: tag ancestry/dates/links/test counts/TSan and mutation math verified; bash -n, shellcheck, preflight positive/negative probes, wrong-tag refusal, K10 rustdoc, diff check, and changelog structural validation pass. The 02eef5e GitHub link is expected to return 404 only until the local commit is eventually pushed with operator authorization.
close_reason: Release history and K10 delivery status now match tag/source truth, distinguish post-release hardening, and are guarded by fail-closed preflight checks.

title: [DRVQA25][P1][cursor] Retire borrowed-stream cursor on callback and speculative-fetch errors
description:
Finding

Connection::for_each_row_ref marks the query cursor in use during the initial execute, but every post-execute fallible edge uses a bare question-mark return: first-page callbacks, speculative fetch request, response read/decode, next-page request, and borrowed-page callbacks. The sole release_cursor call is on the success tail. A callback error before prefetch therefore leaves the cached cursor in_use. More seriously, a callback error after the next speculative request leaves recovery correctly marked InFlight, so the next operation BREAK/drains the stranded response, but no code retires the cursor from statement_cache, in_use_cursors, copied_cursors, cursor_columns, or lob_prefetch_cursors. Repeating the same SQL parses copied cursors and can accumulate server/client cursor state.

Evidence

crates/oracledb/src/lib.rs for_each_row_ref: initial and paged callback calls, fetch_rows_request, and fetch_rows_ref_response all propagate with question mark; release_cursor(cursor_id) exists only after the loop. fetch_rows_request deliberately leaves recovery InFlight until the response is consumed. Existing cancellation tests prove wire cleanup, not cursor-registry cleanup. The DRVQA25 row-stream cursor fix covers OwnedRowStream, Rows, scroll, and define/fetch paths but does not touch for_each_row_ref. Tracker search found no exact lifecycle issue.

Impact

User callback errors and fetch/cancellation failures can leave an otherwise reusable connection with a permanently in-use cached cursor. Same-SQL reuse then parses copies; repeated failures cause cache churn, client registry growth, close backlog, and eventual ORA-01000 risk. Wire recovery prevents immediate protocol desynchronization, but does not repair lifecycle ownership.

Proposed fix

After the initial execute yields cursor_id, route every subsequent Result through fail-closed cursor retirement. On any callback, request, response, decode, or cancellation error, evict the cursor from cache/registries and queue exactly one close. Preserve normal release_cursor only on complete success. If a speculative response is outstanding, keep the existing deferred BREAK/drain recovery ordering: local retirement may queue the close, but the next request must drain the stranded response before emitting the close piggyback.

Guard tests

Deterministically prove: first-page callback failure evicts cache/in-use/metadata/LOB state and queues one close without wire recovery; paged callback failure after speculative next-request leaves recovery pending, then the next operation BREAK/drains before sending a deduplicated close and remains reusable; response EOF/server error retires the cursor; cancellation before request versus after request preserves correct recovery phase while bounding registries; same-SQL retry does not create copied-cursor accumulation; success still releases to cache rather than closing.
notes:
Verified true with a regression written and run before the fix. Pre-fix command: CARGO_INCREMENTAL=0 cargo test -p oracledb --lib tests::borrowed_stream_first_page_callback_error_retires_cursor -- --exact --nocapture. It failed at the in_use_cursors assertion after the callback error, proving the cached cursor remained owned and reusable state was stale.

Implementation: BorrowedStreamCursorGuard is installed immediately after the initial execute returns cursor_id. Normal completion disarms it through release_cursor. Every post-execute callback, request, response, decode, cancellation, panic unwind, and whole-future drop now runs Drop and close_cursor, evicting statement_cache, in_use_cursors, copied_cursors, cursor_columns, and lob_prefetch_cursors while queuing one deduplicated close. The guard does not alter recovery: InFlight or BreakSent remains armed so ensure_clean_before_request drains before a later request emits the close piggyback.

Compliance production-path guards now drive Connection::for_each_row_ref itself over deterministic loopback packets. borrowed_stream_paged_callback_error_drains_before_close_and_reuse proves the next speculative FETCH is received before the page callback fails, leaves InFlight, evicts every registry, queues one close, and requires BREAK then stranded DATA then RESET then trailing cancel before accepting a reuse DATA packet prefixed by the exact one-id close piggyback. borrowed_stream_drop_after_fetch_request_drains_before_close_and_reuse explicitly polls the method through execute and FETCH, drops the pending future, proves BreakSent cleanup, then proves the same drain and close ordering. borrowed_stream_cancel_before_fetch_request_retires_without_wire_write cancels from the execute-page callback and proves no FETCH byte or sequence allocation. borrowed_stream_malformed_and_truncated_fetches_retire_before_reuse covers fully framed unknown-message and truncated-row-header decode errors, Ready recovery, no spurious BREAK, exact close prefix, and reuse. borrowed_stream_fetch_eof_retires_cursor covers transport EOF while waiting for the continuation. The original first-page callback test still proves immediate cleanup and fresh same-SQL retry without copied-cursor accumulation. Every error case calls close_cursor again and asserts the close queue remains exactly one.

Verification: focused borrowed_stream suite 6 of 6 passed in five consecutive runs; cargo test -p oracledb --lib passed 262 of 262; cargo clippy -p oracledb --lib -- -D warnings passed; lib rustfmt check and diff check passed; live prefetch_overlap passed 4 of 4 against local Oracle including 20k-row identity and mid-prefetch drop/reuse; live tracing stream test passed. A broader all-target clippy attempt is independently baseline-blocked by 232 pre-existing test unwrap and items-after-test diagnostics outside this bead. Workspace fmt was temporarily blocked only by a concurrent SODA edit; this bead lib.rs is formatted. No commit and no bead close per parent handoff.

title: [DRVQA25][P1][SODA] Retire failed define and fetch cursors
description:
Finding

The SODA query layer opens cached server cursors through execute_with_bind_rows_and_options_core, which records query cursor ids in the statement cache, in_use_cursors, cursor_columns and LOB-prefetch registries. Two SODA continuations bypass the driver fail-closed lifecycle rule introduced by DRVQA25.3: execute_collect_with_binds maps an initial DEFINE-FETCH error directly to SodaError, and SodaCursor::fetch_more maps both plain FETCH and DEFINE-FETCH errors directly. The failed cursor therefore remains cache-reusable even though its server-side validity is no longer proven.

Independent reproduction

Offline loopback tests open a synthetic SODA cursor, seed the real Connection cache/ownership registries, send a real FETCH or DEFINE-FETCH request, inject a malformed terminal response, and inspect the post-error state. Both regressions fail on unchanged code because the SODA cursor remains non-terminal and its id remains in all registries instead of being queued once for close.

Impact

Repeated failures can retain in-use/copied cursors until ORA-01000 pressure. A subsequent identical SODA query can also interact with stale cached state. This is fail-open resource ownership after a protocol, cancellation, I/O or server error.

Proposed fix

Route initial SODA define bootstrap failures and continuation fetch/define failures through Connection::close_cursor_on_error. Make SodaCursor terminal on continuation failure so callers cannot retry a cursor already queued for close. Preserve successful ownership and explicit close behavior.

Guard tests

1. Malformed plain FETCH response removes the id from statement cache, in-use, column and LOB registries, queues exactly one close, and marks SodaCursor closed.
2. The same assertions for DEFINE-FETCH.
3. A direct initial define-bootstrap error exercises the shared fail-closed mapper.
4. Successful construction/refill and idempotent registry cleanup remain unchanged.

Acceptance

Focused SODA tests, oracledb lib tests with soda enabled, fmt, targeted clippy, diff check and fresh-eyes compliance pass are green. No core row-stream, Arrow or documentation changes are required.
notes:
VERIFIED TRUE. Pre-fix offline loopback regressions independently failed for both plain FETCH and DEFINE-FETCH: after a malformed terminal response SodaCursor remained non-terminal and the cursor id stayed in statement_cache, in_use_cursors, cursor_columns and LOB metadata rather than being queued once for close.

Implemented a shared SODA finish_cursor_operation wrapper over Connection::close_cursor_on_error. execute_collect_with_binds now retires the initial describe-only cursor when DEFINE-FETCH fails. SodaCursor::fetch_more retires failed plain/define refills and marks itself terminal. A cancellation already pending before the refill is checked separately and preserves the valid retryable cursor without sending bytes.

Deterministic guards drive real loopback TTC traffic: malformed plain FETCH; malformed DEFINE-FETCH; valid execute plus native-JSON DESCRIBE followed by malformed initial DEFINE-FETCH; a well-formed successful scalar refill proving cache/in-use ownership remains until explicit close; and a pending-cancellation no-wire case. Registry assertions cover statement cache, in-use, column/LOB metadata and exactly-one close; repeated retirement remains deduplicated.

Final local verification: all 5 lifecycle guards pass; cargo test -p oracledb --features soda --lib passes 303/303; cargo clippy -p oracledb --features soda --lib -- -D warnings passes; live_soda integration target compiles; workspace fmt and diff check pass. An all-test-target clippy attempt is not a usable baseline and reports 239 pre-existing test lint violations outside this bead. UBS targeted source/diff scans ran; source heuristics were triaged as false positives or test-only (hex_decode mislabeled JWT bypass, RAII sockets, test expect/assert, intentional clones). UBS build/check/clippy/audit/deny subgates were green. Awaiting independent final compliance verdict; no commit and no close per parent handoff.
close_reason: Verified and fixed: every failed SODA define/fetch retires its cursor exactly once and successful ownership semantics remain intact.

title: [DRVQA25][P1][SODA] Release one-shot query cursor ownership
description:
Finding

SODA methods that consume a SELECT into owned values never release the query cursor recorded by Connection::execute_query_with_bind_rows_and_options_core. SodaCollection::get_count and get_one return scalars/documents, get_documents drains then drops SodaCursor, and SodaDatabase::open_collection/get_collection_names return owned metadata/names. None exposes a cursor handle or calls release_cursor. The cursor id therefore remains in in_use_cursors after the public method returns.

Independent reproduction

A real loopback TTC regression calls SodaCollection::get_one twice with the same SQL and valid two-column execute responses. On unchanged code both documents decode correctly, but the final assertion fails because the original cursor id is still in in_use_cursors; the second call treats the cached statement as busy and enters the copied-cursor path instead of reusing one released cached cursor.

False-positive check

This is not the explicit SodaCursor API, where the caller owns close. These one-shot methods return only owned values and give the caller no cursor id to release. Generic query_one/query_all facades release on completion/drop. Server end-of-data does not repair the client in-use/cache registry.

Impact

Repeated one-shot SODA reads leave cached cursors permanently busy, force fresh parses/copies for identical SQL, grow copied/close state, and can eventually contribute to ORA-01000 and avoidable parse pressure. Local row-conversion errors can leak ownership too.

Proposed fix

Release each consumed QueryResult cursor immediately after its owned rows/out values are available, before local decoding can fail. Add an idempotent internal SodaCursor ownership-release path and make get_documents invoke it on success and every error; failed fetch cursors already retired by DRVQA25.8 remain closed and must not be released back to cache. Preserve explicit open_cursor ownership.

Guard tests

1. Repeated get_one over real TTC responses reuses one cached cursor, leaves in_use/copied empty and queues no close.
2. One-shot count and database open/list owned-result paths release on success and local decode failure.
3. get_documents releases after normal drain and row-conversion failure, while a failed fetch remains retired rather than cache-released.
4. open_cursor still retains ownership until explicit close.

Acceptance

Focused one-shot lifecycle tests, all SODA lifecycle guards, oracledb lib with soda enabled, live_soda compile, production clippy, fmt/diff and independent compliance review pass. No raw execute semantics or non-SODA query APIs change.
notes: VERIFIED TRUE AND FIXED. Pre-fix real TTC loopback: two successful SodaCollection::get_one calls left cursor 117 in in_use_cursors and forced the busy/copied path; the assertion failed on unchanged production code. Fix: get_count, get_one, SodaDatabase::open_collection, and get_collection_names release consumed QueryResult ownership before local decoding. get_documents invokes idempotent internal SodaCursor::release on drain/error; fetch failures already retired by DRVQA25.8 stay closed and are never returned to cache. Public close preserves double-close behavior. Passing real TTC guards: repeated get_one and count cache reuse; getDocuments drain release; malformed continuation stays retired exactly once; repeated database open/list reuse; invalid descriptor releases before local JSON failure; public open_cursor remains owned after drain until explicit close. Final shared-snapshot gates: cargo test -p oracledb --features soda --lib 317/317; cargo clippy -p oracledb --features soda --lib -- -D warnings; live_soda test target no-run; fmt and diff checks. UBS scanned collection/cursor/database; embedded cargo check, test build, clippy, audit, deny all green. UBS critical hex_decode reports are false JWT heuristics; warnings are test-only expects/asserts, RAII loopback sockets, guarded indexes, and required ownership clones. Static call-site audit confirms every SODA SELECT returning owned values is covered; non-query insert/remove/replace/create/drop/DDL paths never enter in_use_cursors. Awaiting independent fresh-eyes verdict; do not close or commit per root instruction.
close_reason: Verified and fixed: all one-shot SODA query paths release cursor ownership while explicit streaming ownership remains caller-controlled.

title: Encrypted ewallet.pem PKCS8 decrypt + standalone ewallet.p12 wallet format (deferred from x1p)
description:
Deferred follow-up split out of rust-oracledb-x1p on 2026-06-29. x1p ships only the docs/SUPPORT matrix + typed format diagnostics + redaction sweep in Release 0.5.1. THIS bead is the heavy other half: actually broadening wallet format support. Target 0.5.2.

REAL GAPS (from GH #6, with verified current state):
a) Encrypted ewallet.pem PKCS8 private keys are NOT decrypted even when wallet_password is supplied -- today oracledb-protocol/src/tls/wallet.rs returns a typed error pointing the user to orapki -auto_login or cwallet.sso. Add real PKCS8 decryption when wallet_password is present.
b) No standalone ewallet.p12 wallet format. The PKCS12 parser (oracledb-protocol/src/tls/pfx.rs) is currently private/internal to cwallet.sso (tls/sso.rs, experimental feature). Expose a supported standalone p12 path reusing pfx.rs.

REQS: crypto via RustCrypto (unsafe-free, keeps #![forbid(unsafe_code)]); typed/machine-classifiable errors for still-unsupported shapes; redact wallet paths + passwords in ALL formatted output; tests for encrypted-wallet handling + unsupported-format diagnostics; update the docs/SUPPORT matrix x1p creates to flip these rows from unsupported to supported. Full server acceptance for ADB wallets needs real Autonomous Database infra -- flag honestly what is verified offline vs live.
notes: FIELD CONFIRMATION 2026-07-03 (urgency raised to P1): a fresh-install field test of the downstream MCP server found OCI ADB profiles blocked because the default ADB wallet zip ships cwallet.sso + ewallet.p12, and only unencrypted ewallet.pem connects today; users must hand-extract a PEM. python-oracledb thin reads ewallet.p12 + wallet_password natively - that is the parity bar. (Supersedes duplicate bead rust-oracledb-wallet-p12-sso-facf, closed as dup.)
close_reason: Landed: PKCS8 encrypted ewallet.pem decrypts with wallet_password (PBES2/PBKDF2 SHA1+SHA256/AES-CBC, RustCrypto only); standalone ewallet.p12 first-class via the internal PFX reader; cwallet.sso promoted to always-on after verification against a REAL orapki 23.26 wallet fixture byte-identical to its paired p12; typed fail-closed WalletError variants (Pkcs12/KeyDecrypt/PasswordRequired, non_exhaustive additive - semver-checks minor-clean vs 0.7.0); loader precedence pem->p12->sso; fuzz target extended; SUPPORT.md/TLS_SETUP.md updated honestly (offline proven, live ADB pending). An untouched ADB wallet zip now connects directly.

title: [bug] AQ RAW/JSON dequeue silently truncates when declared image_length > wire bytes
description: E8 R2 Pass E. aq.rs:1036 parse_aq_deq_response returned available bytes even when fewer than declared image_length (silent truncation). FIXED: validate declared length against available -> TtcDecode on shortfall (RAW+JSON). Malformed + exact-length regressions.
close_reason: STALE — FIXED in E8 R2 but bead left open. Verified in current code: aq.rs parse_aq_deq_response reads image_length (read_ub4) and calls limits().check_response_bytes(image_length) [:1028-1029], so a declared length exceeding the available wire bytes is rejected as TtcDecode instead of silently truncating (RAW + JSON). Regressions present: raw_dequeue_rejects_declared_image_length_shortfall [:1154], json_dequeue_rejects_declared_image_length_shortfall [:1162], raw_dequeue_accepts_exact_declared_image_length [:1170]. Closing as resolved.

title: transient 'unknown TTC message type 129' under default-parallel live tests on small container
description: Confirmed environment/contention-sensitive [NOT a code defect]. live_typed_roundtrip_matrix_e12 passes 3/3 in ISOLATION but intermittently fails inside the full suite [observed under both default-parallel load AND once in a serial --include-ignored run]. The driver itself is correct [conformance 2578=2578 0 regressions; full borrowed+owned fetch clean]. Root pattern: the #[ignore] live tests run against a single small FREEPDB1 dev container and are sensitive to load + container/session state + test ordering. BROADER 1.0 ACTION: provision a properly-resourced DB and wire a LIVE-TEST LANE into CI [the #[ignore] tests are currently outside the gate, so they accumulate context-dependent failures AND real regressions go uncaught — bur7 was a real bug that sat undetected precisely because its test is #[ignore]]. Then make the contention-sensitive tests robust [controlled isolation / retries].
close_reason: ROOT-CAUSED to a REAL driver bug (the earlier 'environment/contention-sensitive, NOT a code defect' conclusion was wrong). The matrix re-executes a cached cursor (select :1 as v from dual) whose column type changed CHAR->CLOB; the server re-describes it as CLOB but streams the value in LONG form (adjust_refetch_metadata folds CLOB->LONG), which carries the LONG status trailer (null-indicator sb4 + return-code ub4 = ORA-01405). The EXECUTE parse path runs with fetch_long_status=false, so the 5-byte trailer was left unconsumed and the next message byte 0x81 was mis-read as 'unknown TTC message type 129 at position 95'. Intermittent because the adjust only fires when the cold server re-describes mid-response. FIX: fetch.rs promotes fetch_long_status when adjust_refetch_metadata folds a column to LONG/LONG RAW, so parse_row_data consumes the trailer (covers CLOB->LONG and BLOB->LONG RAW). Offline regression query_response_consumes_long_trailer_for_clob_refetched_as_long (real 23ai wire capture; red-green verified). Verified: cold-bootstrap repro 0/18 fail post-fix (was 1/1); full live lane 5/5 green with the test re-included.

Forcing either would be the opposite of careful. Instead documented the panic-safety posture + the deliberate decision in docs/SAFETY_AUDIT.md section 9 [active gates, decode-core audit, running byte limits, 19-target fuzz 0 crashes], and corrected the now-stale deny.toml note in that doc. Active gates remain: unsafe_code=forbid, unwrap_used=deny, todo=deny, dbg_macro=deny.

title: [bug] query_one/query_opt falsely return TooManyRows for single-row LONG (and any arraysize=1) results
description:
FOUND BY W3-E1.2 live typed matrix. query_one / query_opt over a LONG (LONG RAW) column return Error::TooManyRows even when the query yields exactly one row; collecting the same query returns one row with the correct value.

ROOT CAUSE (lib.rs:2463-2479, Rows::one()/opt()): the cardinality test is
    let too_many = self.more_rows || self.batch.len() > 1;
But more_rows=true means 'server has NOT confirmed end-of-data', not '>1 row'. For normal columns the default arraysize (lib.rs:1621, prefetch=arraysize) is large, so a 1-row result returns more_rows=false and one() works. LONG/LONG RAW require a per-row define-fetch round trip (statement._requires_define, lib.rs:7082/9908), so the fetch is effectively single-row and the server returns 1 row with more_rows=true -> one()/opt() misread that as TooManyRows. The same defect hits any query where arraysize/prefetch is 1 (LONG/LOB, or user-set arraysize(1)).

FIX: do not treat more_rows as definitive cardinality. one()/opt() must confirm: if exactly one row is in hand and more_rows is set, fetch the next batch; only TooManyRows if a second row actually materializes (batch.len() reaches 2 or a follow-up batch is non-empty). Bounded to at most one extra fetch in the arraysize=1 case. Because one()/opt() are sync (no cx), the confirmation must happen in the async query_one/query_opt path (fetch-ahead to >=2 rows or end-of-data) before the cardinality check. Add a regression test (ignored live test for LONG + an offline scripted-transport test forcing more_rows=true on a single row).

Severity P1: query_one is a core 1.0 API (four-family cardinality helper); must be fixed before 1.0. NOT yet fixed (E1.2 worked around it with collect for LONG/LONG RAW).
close_reason: FIXED. query_one/query_opt now fetch ahead (private materialize_for_cardinality, <=1 extra round trip, only when one row in hand with more_rows set) before the cardinality check, so a single-row LONG/LONG RAW result returns the row instead of TooManyRows. Preserves the LONG/LOB continuation key (clones row1, keeps original in batch for next_batch). Live LONG + LONG RAW query_one regressions added to live_typed.rs; clippy + workspace + live green. Direct Rows::one()/opt() on an arraysize=1 stream keep materialized-batch semantics (noted for W4-T1).

title: Statement cache reuses stale bind metadata when a rebind changes the bind TYPE (ORA-01722)
description:
Found while building the version-matrix full suite (matrix_full.rs), reproduced on XE 18/21 and FREE 23ai alike (not version-specific).

Repro: execute 'select :1 from dual' with an i64/NUMBER bind (works), then re-execute the SAME SQL text on the same connection with a Text bind ('naïve-Ω-…'). The statement cache reuses the cached cursor's bind metadata (NUMBER), the server coerces the text and fails with ORA-01722 invalid number. The reference (python-oracledb) re-describes binds when the bind type changes on a cached statement, so this sequence works there — a real parity gap.

Expected: a rebind with a different value type must invalidate/re-describe the cached statement's bind metadata (or key the cache on bind type shape as the reference effectively does).

Workaround used in examples/matrix_full.rs: distinct SQL text per bind type ('select :1 as v_text from dual'). See the comment there; remove it once fixed and add a regression test that reuses one SQL text across NUMBER->TEXT->RAW binds.
close_reason: Fixed: statement cache now records the bind TYPE shape (family+csfrm, untyped-NULL wildcard) per cached cursor; a rebind with an incompatible shape drops the cursor (close piggyback) and forces a fresh PARSE with the new bind metadata. Ground truth established first: python-oracledb 4.0.1 sends a full EXECUTE (no re-parse) on binds_changed and gets the SAME ORA-01722 from the server (its 932/1007 retry does not cover 1722), so the robust fix is shape-keyed invalidation, not reference mimicry. Changes: crates/oracledb/src/lib.rs (CachedStatement/BindShapeSlot, bind_type_shape/bind_shape_is_compatible, cache get/take/put/insert + execute path), matrix_full.rs workaround removed (VARCHAR2 probe reuses 'select :1 from dual'). Tests: regression live test rebind_type_change_on_cached_statement_redescribes (NUMBER->TEXT->RAW->NUMBER, one SQL text) reproduced ORA-01722 before fix, green after on xe18 AND free23; 4 new unit tests (shape compat, size-change stays compatible, null wildcard, executemany inference, insert slot inheritance); all 153 lib tests green; matrix full xe18 green.

title: [FOLLOWUP] Report asupersync 0.3.4 short-timer wheel bug upstream (fires only on 1st timed use/process)
description: asupersync 0.3.4 time::timeout/Sleep wheel delivers a short timer's wakeup only for the FIRST timed use in a process; subsequent short timers never fire (deterministic 1st-pass/2nd-fail). Sleep::with_time_getter does NOT force the OS-thread fallback when an ambient driver exists (sleep.rs:483/535/624; timeout_future.rs:231). We worked around it in the driver with a std-thread watchdog deadline (TimedAcquireDeadline, pool.rs) for the pool TIMEDWAIT acquire. File a minimal upstream repro + issue. Low priority; driver is robust regardless. See memory asupersync-short-timer-wheel-limitation. discovered-from rust-oracledb-sner.
notes: 2026-07-06: root cause VERIFIED + minimal repro CONFIRMED + upstream report DRAFTED (docs/upstream/asupersync-0.3.4-short-timer.md). Repro (standalone, asupersync=0.3.4, current_thread runtime, 3x50ms time::timeout): deterministically hangs on the 2nd short timer — 'BUG REPRODUCED, a short timer after the first never fired'. Root cause confirmed in sleep.rs: Sleep::poll prefers the ambient TimerDriverHandle and tears down the OS-thread fallback (sleep.rs ~535 request_stop_fallback; fallback only spawned when no driver, ~624); with_time_getter does not force the fallback (483/535/624, timeout_future.rs:231), so Sleep inherits the driver wheel's single-fire-per-process defect with no independent wake path. Doc proposes 2 upstream fixes (fix the wheel; and/or don't tear down fallback until the driver has demonstrably armed the short timer). Driver workaround verified sound (TimedAcquireDeadline std-thread park_timeout in pool/acquire.rs; pool+live timeout suites green; long timeouts unaffected). REMAINING: actually submit to the asupersync tracker — needs the maintainer repo URL / submission access this session doesn't have. Bead stays OPEN for that submission step.
close_reason: FILED upstream: https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/asupersync/issues/52. IMPORTANT CORRECTION during re-verification (operator asked 'is it really an issue?' before filing): the original 'subsequent short timers NEVER fire / block_on hangs' characterization was WRONG — it came from a repro built WITHOUT a reactor (RuntimeBuilder::current_thread().build()). With a properly-configured runtime (.with_reactor(create_reactor())), the timers DO fire. The ACTUAL, deterministic bug: after the FIRST timer in a runtime, every subsequent timer has a ~250ms FLOOR — a sub-250ms timeout fires at ~250ms instead of its requested duration; >=250ms is accurate; long timers are NOT fired early. So it's a short-timer precision bug, genuinely real, but a latency/floor issue not a lost-wakeup. Driver workaround (TimedAcquireDeadline std-thread for the short pool-wait timer) stays valid; seconds-scale connect/read timeouts unaffected. docs/upstream/asupersync-0.3.4-short-timer.md corrected with measured table (1/10/50/200ms->250ms floored; 300/500/1000ms accurate).

title: [bug] Query::stream_lobs() over CLOB fails with Protocol(TtcDecode invalid ub8 length)
description: FOUND BY W3-E7.4 live e2e suite (scenario lob_stream_lobs_locator_mode, currently SKIPPED). Query::stream_lobs() (LOB locator streaming mode, opt out of default materialization) over a CLOB column returns Protocol(TtcDecode("invalid ub8 length")). Default Query LOB materialization (read/write via locator) works; only the stream_lobs() path over CLOB fails. Likely a ub8 length decode mismatch in the streamed-LOB define/fetch path for character LOBs. Needs investigation (compare BLOB vs CLOB stream_lobs decode; check the ub8 length field framing for CS_FORM_IMPLICIT). Add a regression once root-caused. To triage in E8/W4-T1.
close_reason: FIXED. LOB column decoder unconditionally read size(ub8)+chunk_size(ub4); those fields exist only in LOB-prefetch (define-fetch) responses, so a plain stream_lobs() locator fetch misaligned read_ub8 onto the locator length prefix (0x72) -> invalid ub8 length. Fix: LobDecodeMode enum (PlainLocator vs DefineMetadata) + per-cursor lob_prefetch_cursors tracking selects the correct shape; BFILE always locator-only. Offline two-shape protocol regression + live e2e lob_stream_lobs_locator_mode re-enabled. Gates + conformance (0 regressions/0 missing) green. Default LOB materialization unchanged.

title: [bug] execute_many RETURNING: BatchOutcome.rows_for exposes only first iteration's value
description: FOUND BY W3-E7.4 live e2e suite. execute_many_with over an array UPDATE ... RETURNING name INTO :3 affecting 2 rows: rows_affected=2, per_row_counts=[1,1], returning().len()=2, but returning().rows_for(2) exposes only 1 value (expected 2). ROOT CAUSE: array DML decodes RETURNING once per iteration (parse_returning_row_data, fetch.rs:869/897 pushes one (bind_index,rows) group per iteration), so return_values=[(2,[v1]),(2,[v2])]. ReturningRows.rows_for(bind_index) uses find_map -> returns only the FIRST matching group. The protocol per-iteration grouping is correct + matches python-oracledb (keep it; pyshim/conformance depends on it). FIX (driver only): BatchOutcome::from_query_result now uses ReturningRows::coalesced() which merges groups sharing a bind index in input order, so rows_for(bind_index) returns every affected row's value. ExecuteOutcome (single execute) already arrives as one group/bind, unchanged. Offline unit regression batch_outcome_coalesces_array_dml_returning_per_bind + live e2e LONG/RETURNING. Conformance unaffected (protocol unchanged).
close_reason: FIXED. BatchOutcome::from_query_result now uses ReturningRows::coalesced() merging per-iteration RETURNING groups that share a bind index, so rows_for(bind) returns every affected row's value. Protocol per-iteration grouping kept (conformance/pyshim unaffected). Offline regression batch_outcome_coalesces_array_dml_returning_per_bind + live e2e (6/6 green).

No code change until then.

title: BUG: thin decoder mis-frames multi-packet WIDE-row results past ~1500 rows (encoded NUMBER too long / truncated TTC payload)
close_reason: Fixed in commit 3cdc451 (branch fix-widerow). Root cause: read_data_response_boundary (crates/oracledb/src/lib.rs) terminated multi-packet reassembly on an UNGUARDED trailing-byte check -- any DATA packet whose post-flags payload merely ended in TNS_MSG_TYPE_END_OF_RESPONSE (29/0x1d) or TNS_MSG_TYPE_FLUSH_OUT_BINDS (19/0x13) was treated as end-of-response, truncating wide multi-packet results. Reference impl/thin/packet.pyx::has_end_of_response guards the 0x1d check on packet_size == HEADER+3 (lone marker byte). Fix: pure data_packet_ends_response() ends the response only on the END_OF_RESPONSE/EOF data flag or a minimal lone-marker packet; flush-out-binds detected from the terminal byte of the reassembled stream. Regression: 3 hermetic unit tests (lib.rs) + live wide_row_multipacket.rs (3000x 20-col rows over 512B SDU) -- all FAIL before / PASS after. Parity sentinels green (1100 57p/5s, 2200 39p, 4300 73p, 8000 82p).

title: [env] stale CQN registration (regid 3755) in the rust-oracledb-free container breaks conformance test_3006
description: Conformance test_3000_subscription.py::test_3006 asserts sub.id == (SELECT REGID FROM USER_CHANGE_NOTIFICATION_REGS) i.e. assumes ONE registration. The shared container has 1 ORPHAN CQN registration (regid 3755) left by this session's CQN test runs (interrupted by spend/rate limits) + Codex's CQN grant work. So fetchone() returns 3755 while the new sub.id is correct (e.g. 3786) -> test fails. NOT a driver code regression (sub.id is correct; no R5 code touches CQN). CANNOT clean: DBMS_CHANGE_NOTIFICATION + DBMS_CQ_NOTIFICATION are both unavailable in the gvenzl/oracle-free 23ai image (ORA-06550), and the orphan's protocol details for a wire-level unregister are unknown. RESOLUTION: clears on container restart / fresh DB, or via a wire-level subscribe_unregister of regid 3755 if its namespace/client_id are recovered. Re-verify conformance on a clean container. The committed code is 0-code-regression (2577 pass).
close_reason: RESOLVED — orphan cleared. Verified free23 (rust-oracledb-free) now has ZERO change-notification registrations: 'select count(*) from dba_change_notification_regs' = 0 (the orphan regid 3755 is gone; the container was restarted during the hardening campaign). The bead's own resolution criterion was 'clears on container restart / fresh DB', now true. The failure was solely the stale orphan making USER_CHANGE_NOTIFICATION_REGS return 3755 instead of the new sub.id (sub.id was always correct; no driver code regression). With 0 pre-existing registrations, test_3006's single-registration assumption holds. CI was never affected (CI uses fresh containers). Not running python-oracledb's full thick-mode conformance harness — disproportionate for a confirmed-cleared P3 env chore.

title: L2 post-auth cassettes: extend beyond typed query to LOB/AQ/DPL/CQN round-trips
notes: Same slice+seeded-loopback mechanism as cwsr (crates/oracledb/src/version_cassettes.rs postauth harness). For each op, capture connect+auth+op live, slice off the connect+auth prefix, commit only the deterministic secret-free op frames, and replay offline against a loopback Connection seeded from the manifest (caps + post-auth ttc_seq_num). Server-assigned ids (LOB locators, cursor ids, AQ msgids) travel back in recorded responses so replay reproduces them; the client-side seed carries only caps+seq. Watch for: LOB read chunking multi-round-trip framing; AQ enqueue/dequeue payload determinism; DPL direct-path stream; CQN registration. Generalize the manifest/lane list; reuse loopback_for_replay + reencode_frames + the secret scan. Keep each op secret-free and offline-replayable per-PR.

title: Support thin external wallet auth without username/password
description: oraclemcp W4 removed thick mode and now fails external_auth profiles explicitly because ConnectOptions requires user/password. Add or scope thin support for external wallet/OS-style auth where a wallet or Oracle Net config supplies credentials and no username/password is passed. Acceptance: public ConnectOptions can represent the no-credential mode without dummy secrets; tests cover auto-login wallet/alias shape or a documented unsupported server-side limit; errors must not expose wallet paths or credential material.
acceptance_criteria: 0.5.1 deliverable (TYPED SURFACE ONLY -- no heavy backend): public ConnectOptions can express passwordless/external auth intent WITHOUT dummy user/password; a typed, machine-classifiable AuthMode + UnsupportedExternalAuth error distinguishes (a) wallet-for-TLS + user/pass [supported today], (b) passwordless external [typed-unsupported, or supported-where-feasible if cheap], (c) unsupported-in-thin; capability metadata is queryable so oraclemcp doctor is precise; no wallet path/password/cert in any formatted or Debug output; unit tests for construction + redaction + failure-classification. Shares the AuthMode spine with rust-oracledb-qm4 -- build it once. Live passwordless auth where Oracle supports it MAY land here if cheap, otherwise documented as a server-side limit. Scoped into Release 0.5.1. GH #2.
notes: Backend stays as-is; only the typed surface ships in 0.5.1. Spinout for deferred work: see kerberos-radius-backends bead rust-oracledb-kerberos-radius-backends-bpsh (Kerberos/RADIUS) — shared AuthMode spine.
close_reason: Added passwordless external-auth ConnectOptions constructor/builder without dummy caller credentials, queryable thin auth capabilities, typed UnsupportedAuthMode(External), and redaction tests for wallet path/password/cert and auth material.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: oraclemcp GH issue #2 (verified): ConnectOptions::new requires user+password positionally (lib.rs:749) -> passwordless external/wallet auth needs dummy creds. WANT: AuthMode::External/WalletExternal OR public capability metadata so 'oraclemcp doctor' distinguishes (a) wallet-for-TLS + user/pass [SUPPORTED today], (b) passwordless wallet/external [GAP], (c) unsupported-in-thin. title: Fix TZ-aware TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE binds losing offset (python-oracledb #374)
description: Confirmed (adversarially verified) defect inherited from python-oracledb thin mode (their #374, still OPEN upstream). Our TIMESTAMP_TZ encoder crates/oracledb-protocol/src/thin/codecs.rs:57-78 unconditionally writes the fixed (20,60)=+00:00 marker and takes no offset argument; BindValue::Timestamp (crates/oracledb-protocol/src/thin/types.rs:509-518) has no field to carry a UTC offset. Result: a timezone-aware datetime bound to a TSTZ column is stored as if UTC (wall-clock kept, offset dropped) — the instant is misrepresented. FIX: add a signed offset_minutes field to BindValue::Timestamp (default 0 for naive/LTZ/plain), thread the tz offset through ToSql for tz-aware chrono types, and have encode_oracle_timestamp_tz write buf[11]=TZ_HOUR_OFFSET+offset_hours, buf[12]=TZ_MINUTE_OFFSET+offset_mins. Add a round-trip test (bind +05:30, read back, assert instant preserved). NOTE: fixing this is a differentiator — python-oracledb still has the bug.
close_reason: NOT A BUG (verified). python-oracledb #374 is a duck-typing silent-corruption: passing a tz-aware datetime, the offset is ignored. In our STATICALLY-typed API this is structurally impossible: there is NO ToSql for any tz-aware type (FixedOffset/Utc/Local — grep-empty across crates), and ToSql for NaiveDateTime always emits ora_type_num=180 (DB_TYPE_TIMESTAMP), never TSTZ (181), so encode_oracle_timestamp_tz is never reached for a value bind. A user cannot supply an offset for us to drop. This is a MISSING FEATURE (tz-aware datetime bind support), not a correctness bug. Filed nothing to fix; tz-aware bind support can be a future enhancement if desired.

title: [bug] dirty-wire: read_lob/commit/rollback/ping/AQ/change_password/subscribe skip ensure_clean_before_request after dropped future
description: E8 R3 (3/3). CancelDrainGuard::drop marks BreakSent without sending BREAK; next op must ensure_clean_before_request to break+drain. fetch/execute paths do; read_lob(lib.rs:6253), commit, rollback, ping, AQ enq/deq, change_password, CQN subscribe/unsubscribe do NOT -> issue request on dirty wire -> desync. FIX: audit all request-sending methods + add ensure_clean_before_request. ScriptedTransport regression.
close_reason: FIXED. ensure_clean_before_request added to all 25 request-sending methods (read_lob/commit/rollback/ping/AQ/change_password/subscribe/sessionless/tpc/scroll/direct-path/pipeline/...); dropped cancellable future no longer desyncs next op. Ref protocol.pyx/connection.pyx/queue.pyx/lob.pyx/subscr.pyx. ScriptedTransport regression.

Define the lossiness boundary explicitly (f64<->decimal NUMBER documented-lossy) rather than burying it in prop_assume. Effort M.

title: Move bind/SQL driver logic out of PyO3 shim
description: External assessment correctly identified that substantial SQL/bind/type-conversion behavior still lives in crates/oracledb-pyshim/src/lib.rs. Migrate reusable driver logic into oracledb-protocol/oracledb where it belongs, leaving the PyO3 shim as marshalling and Python surface adaptation only. Candidate areas: SQL bind/output classification, executemany row shaping, type-conversion metadata, DML RETURNING/object projection helpers, and output/input variable aggregation contracts. Acceptance should keep reference tests green and avoid fake parity or Python-shaped Rust.

Purity achieved: the last driver-logic helpers (identifier quoting rule + ALTER SESSION parsing) moved to oracledb-protocol::sql with unit tests (commit cf09467); live connection+LOB regression 99 passed/5 skip. Audited remaining steps already meet the no-driver-logic-in-shim bar: AQ wire decode in protocol::thin::aq (shim builds Python objects only); LOB wire ops in driver read_lob_with_timeout + text codec in protocol (d49 slice H); ThinConnState is python-oracledb's connection-attribute contract (adapter state, not driver logic). fake_parity_scan CLEAN. Only genuine remainder is nto's thread-offload bridge (harness-only, tracked separately).

title: [parity-audit] Upstream python-oracledb open-issue audit follow-ups (2026-06-29)
description:
Parent tracker for follow-ups from the 2026-06-29 audit of all 24 OPEN issues on github.com/oracle/python-oracledb against our thin port (6 read-only investigators, file:line evidence; reference vendored at v4.0.1). Goal: avoid "bug parity" -- faithfully reproducing upstream thin bugs -- and convert our structural advantages into guarded, documented wins.

Full standings live in docs/PARITY_LEDGER.md (the living ledger; keep it updated going forward, including the intentional-divergence register for any fix that changes wire output vs upstream's buggy behavior).

Children (all labeled upstream-parity):
- #579 IAM-token connect-descriptor fidelity (we are currently WORSE than upstream).
- #502 bound the connect handshake reads (unbounded ACCEPT/AUTH read = hang).
- #374/#274 offset-preserving TSTZ/LTZ both directions (surpass; additive on fetch, wire-divergent on bind).
- #376 thin TPC timeout inherited -- live regression + tracking (HOLD for parity).
- Immunity regression tests for #595/#400/#596/#422 (lock in the advantages).

Out of scope / parity feature-gaps (documented in SUPPORT.md, surpass-later, not bugs): #111 LDAP, #253 SOCKS, #318 sharding, #324 compression, #366 config-provider, #564 DPL-into-partition. Already tracked: Kerberos #82/#381 (qm4), #592 (mwu). Not applicable: #398/#122/#302/#594.

Suggested release targeting: #579 + #502 are the highest-value actionable bugs (target an early 0.5.x patch); TSTZ + immunity tests follow; #376 stays a tracked live test.
close_reason: Completed for 0.5.1: all five child follow-ups are closed with evidence (#579 descriptor fidelity, #502 bounded connect handshake reads, #374/#274 offset-preserving TSTZ, #376 HOLD tracking live regression, and #595/#400/#596/#422 immunity tests). Out-of-scope feature gaps remain documented/tracked as deferred beads.

title: [parity] #579 IAM-token connect-descriptor fidelity: inject TOKEN_AUTH=OCI_TOKEN + forward SECURITY/TCPS passthrough
description:
Upstream python-oracledb #579 (thin, OPEN at v4.0.1): IAM-token thin connects to an ADB-S Serverless PRIVATE ENDPOINT fail with DPY-6005/ORA-12506 because TOKEN_AUTH=OCI_TOKEN is not present in the TNS Connect descriptor the listener sees (the PE listener does ACL/routing on the initial connect packet). Thick, JDBC Thin, and SQL*Plus all connect.

VERIFIED CURRENT STATE (we are WORSE than upstream thin):
- Our connect descriptor builder lib.rs:8106 (listener_connect_descriptor_with_server) and lib.rs:8117 (auth_connect_descriptor) emit ONLY (DESCRIPTION=(ADDRESS=(PROTOCOL=tcp)(HOST..)(PORT..))(CONNECT_DATA=(SERVICE_NAME..)(CID..))). No SECURITY section at all; TOKEN_AUTH never emitted anywhere.
- We hardcode PROTOCOL=tcp even for TCPS, and omit SSL_SERVER_DN_MATCH / SSL params.
- The connect path collapses the parsed Description to a 4-field EasyConnect (lib.rs:2142, net/mod.rs:41-99), so a user-supplied (SECURITY=(...)(TOKEN_AUTH=OCI_TOKEN)) in the DSN is parsed (builders.rs:327-335 Security.extra) then DISCARDED before packet build -- the manual escape hatch is dead. Upstream thin DOES forward extra_security_args on TCPS (connect_params.pyx:819-913).

WORK (two independent parts):
P1. When token auth is configured (options.access_token.is_some(), which already requires TLS), inject a SECURITY segment with TOKEN_AUTH=OCI_TOKEN into BOTH the listener and auth connect descriptors. Verify the exact token value/casing JDBC Thin uses before shipping. This is the actual ADB-S Private Endpoint fix and goes BEYOND upstream thin.
P2. Stop collapsing to the 4-field EasyConnect for descriptor serialization: carry the full Description/Security (incl. .extra) through a real build_connect_string-style serializer (port reference connect_params.pyx:819-913), emitting SECURITY on TCPS with extra verbatim and the correct PROTOCOL=tcps + SSL params. Restores manual injection and fixes the hardcoded-tcp/missing-SSL gap. Overlaps clvm (transport fidelity) and our IAM work (5bh delivered, cco follow-up).

TEST PLAN (testing-golden-artifacts + testing-conformance-harnesses + testing-real-service-e2e-no-mocks):
- Golden wire-byte artifact: serialize the connect packet for a token-auth TCPS config; assert the SECURITY section contains TOKEN_AUTH=OCI_TOKEN, PROTOCOL=tcps, and SSL_SERVER_DN_MATCH; scrub dynamic CID (PROGRAM/HOST/USER) via the scrubber registry. UPDATE_GOLDENS workflow + PROVENANCE note.
- Differential/round-trip: assert our serialized descriptor re-parses (EasyConnect/Description::parse) back to an equivalent Description incl. the SECURITY extras (no field dropped). Compare shape to reference connect_params.pyx output where feasible.
- Conformance gate: harness/run.sh baseline|rust|diff must remain 0 regressions (adding SECURITY only when token auth is set must not perturb the non-token paths the suite exercises).
- Live no-mock (#[ignore]): against the 23ai container, confirm a normal TCPS connect still succeeds with the new SECURITY segment present. Full ADB-S Private Endpoint validation needs real OCI infra -- flag honestly as un-run in CI; coordinate with oraclemcp for a real-endpoint check.
ACCEPTANCE: TOKEN_AUTH emitted for token auth; arbitrary SECURITY params forwarded verbatim on TCPS; PROTOCOL=tcps correct; goldens reviewed+committed; 0 conformance regressions; no token/wallet/path leak in any formatted output.
close_reason: Implemented for 0.5.1: token-auth descriptors now serialize TCPS, SECURITY passthrough, SSL_SERVER_DN_MATCH/CERT_DN, and TOKEN_AUTH=OCI_TOKEN while keeping wallet paths client-local. Verified by token_auth_descriptor_uses_tcps_security_and_passthrough, cargo check/clippy/tests, semver-checks, and package verification.

title: [parity] #502 bound the connect handshake reads (unbounded ACCEPT/AUTH read = indefinite hang)
description:
Upstream python-oracledb #502 (OPEN): connect() hangs against an 11g DB reached through an SSH tunnel. Root class in OUR code is broader than 11g: the post-dial handshake reads are UNBOUNDED, so any server that accepts the TCP socket then stalls (half-open tunnel, wedged listener, slow 11g negotiation) hangs the connect forever.

VERIFIED CURRENT STATE:
- TCP dial IS bounded but with a HARDCODED 20s (lib.rs:2154-2158, Duration::from_secs(20)); the parsed DSN tcp_connect_timeout / transport_connect_timeout is IGNORED (SUPPORT.md:236, overlaps clvm F1).
- The ACCEPT and AUTH response reads have NO deadline: lib.rs:2210 (read_packet().await) and lib.rs:2265 (read_data_response().await). A stalled peer after TCP accept => indefinite hang.
- Min supported server is 12.1 (SUPPORT.md:107; pre-12.1 rejected with UnsupportedVersion, TNS floor 300) -- so 11g itself is out of our promised matrix, but the unbounded-read hang is a general robustness defect independent of 11g.

WORK:
- Apply an overall connect DEADLINE that covers the whole handshake (dial + ACCEPT + data/AUTH reads), not just the dial. Honor the DSN-supplied tcp_connect_timeout / transport_connect_timeout when present, else a sane default. Use the asupersync time facility (mind the known 0.3.4 short-timer wheel limitation -- see asupersync-short-timer-wheel-limitation memory; a watchdog deadline may be needed). On expiry return a typed Timeout/connection error and clean up the half-open socket (no leaked task/fd).

TEST PLAN (testing-real-service-e2e-no-mocks -- a REAL local server, no mocking of our code):
- Stall-server test (deterministic): bind a real TcpListener that accept()s then never writes a byte. Assert connect() returns a bounded Timeout error within ~deadline+slack, NOT a hang. Negative control: a listener that completes a normal accept handshake connects fine. Structured JSON-line logging of phase/timing per the skill.
- Slow-after-accept variant: server that sends a partial ACCEPT then stalls -> still bounded.
- DSN-timeout honored: a DSN with a small tcp_connect_timeout fails faster than the default; one with a large value waits longer. Assert the parsed value is actually applied (closes the clvm F1 silent-ignore footgun).
- Cancellation-safety: dropping the connect future mid-handshake leaves no leaked fd/task (reuse the existing cancel-on-drop test harness).
ACCEPTANCE: no connect path can hang indefinitely after TCP accept; tcp_connect_timeout applied; typed timeout error; cancellation-clean; SUPPORT.md updated to remove the "parsed but ignored" caveat for the timeout.
close_reason: Implemented for 0.5.1: connect now honors parsed transport_connect_timeout/tcp_connect_timeout as one overall deadline covering TCP dial, TLS, listener ACCEPT, and AUTH reads; timeout returns typed Error::CallTimeout. Verified by transport_connect_timeout_bounds_post_dial_accept_read plus release gates.

title: [surpass] #374/#274 offset-preserving TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE (bind + fetch)
description:
Surpass-upstream work for the TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE (TSTZ) / WITH LOCAL TIME ZONE (LTZ) family. Addresses upstream python-oracledb #374 (bug, thin+thick, OPEN), #274/#373/#20 (thin, OPEN). Senior-engineer decision (2026-06-29): fix BOTH directions correctly rather than inherit the bug -- a driver that silently corrupts the stored offset is a real correctness defect.

VERIFIED CURRENT STATE (we faithfully inherit both bugs):
- BIND (#374): encode_oracle_timestamp_tz (codecs.rs:57-78) takes only wall-clock components and hardcodes UTC offset bytes TZ_HOUR_OFFSET(20)/TZ_MINUTE_OFFSET(60) at codecs.rs:75-76 -- byte-identical to reference encoders.pyx:329-331. So binding 12:00+05:00 stores 12:00 UTC, dropping +05:00. WORSE: there is NO ToSql for DateTime<Tz>/DateTime<FixedOffset>/DateTime<Utc> at all (sql_convert.rs:1060-1087 only NaiveDateTime->ora_type 180 and NaiveDate), so a Rust caller cannot even express a non-UTC TSTZ bind.
- FETCH (#274/#373/#20): decode (codecs.rs:99-110) reads the offset, applies it once, then returns QueryValue::DateTime with NO tz field; FromSql for NaiveDateTime (sql_convert.rs:387-404) yields a tz-UNAWARE value -- the zone identity parsed at codecs.rs:105-107 is discarded. Named-region zones (TNS_HAS_REGION_ID 0x80) error (codecs.rs:100-104), matching upstream ORA-01805.

WORK:
A. FETCH side (ADDITIVE, parity-SAFE -- no wire change): preserve the numeric offset through the decode and add opt-in FromSql for DateTime<FixedOffset> and DateTime<Utc> that reconstruct the real offset (UTC-normalize for <Utc>). Keep FromSql for NaiveDateTime as today (wall-clock) for back-compat. Optionally surface the offset on QueryValue without breaking the existing enum contract (additive variant or side field). This already SURPASSES python-oracledb thin (which returns naive).
B. BIND side (#374) (WIRE-DIVERGENT from upstream's buggy UTC-zeroing): add ToSql for DateTime<FixedOffset>/<Utc> that writes the REAL offset bytes (off_h+20, off_m+60) so the stored TSTZ keeps its zone. This changes our wire output vs upstream's (buggy) UTC-zeroing -> a deliberate, documented divergence.
C. Named-region zones: keep erroring for now (parity); optionally map a curated set of IANA<->Oracle region ids later (separate effort).

CONFORMANCE GUARD (testing-conformance-harnesses): run harness/run.sh baseline|rust|diff -> must stay 0 regressions. If any upstream test asserts the old UTC-zeroing on bind, record it as an INTENTIONAL DIVERGENCE in docs/PARITY_LEDGER.md (section: Intentional divergences) and treat as XFAIL with rationale, never a silent regression. Most likely upstream has no such test (it is an open bug).

TEST PLAN (testing-metamorphic + testing-golden-artifacts + testing-real-service-e2e-no-mocks):
- Metamorphic round-trip (INVERTIVE MR, proptest): for random instants x with random FixedOffset, assert decode(encode(x)) == x as the SAME instant (UTC-normalized equality), and that two values equal-as-instants but different-offset compare equal after UTC normalization (equivalence MR). Score these MRs >= 2.0; validate with planted mutations (zero-offset, sign-flip on offset, hour/min swap) -> each must be caught.
- Golden wire-byte artifact: encode a known value with a known non-UTC offset; assert the offset bytes are the real (off_h+20, off_m+60), NOT 20/60. Decode-golden for a captured TSTZ/LTZ wire value.
- Arrow consistency MR: for a TSTZ column, assert the Arrow timestamp value equals the row-path value (guards #596 immunity from regressing).
- Live no-mock (#[ignore], 23ai): bind a tz-aware value via DateTime<FixedOffset>, then SELECT to_char(col,'yyyy-mm-dd"T"hh24:mi:ss.fftzh:tzm') and assert the stored offset is preserved (this is the exact #374 repro turned into a passing test). Also fetch it back through FromSql<DateTime<FixedOffset>> and assert instant equality. Structured logging per the skill.
- Negative/edge: year-boundary offsets, +14:00/-12:00 extremes, half-hour and 45-min zones, LTZ vs TZ.
ACCEPTANCE: tz-aware bind preserves offset (live-verified); opt-in tz-aware fetch returns correct FixedOffset/Utc; NaiveDateTime path unchanged; metamorphic MRs pass with mutation-kill evidence; goldens reviewed; 0 conformance regressions or a documented XFAIL divergence in PARITY_LEDGER.md.
close_reason: Implemented for 0.5.1: added offset-preserving QueryValue/QueryValueRef/BindValue::TimestampTz, real fixed-offset TSTZ encode/decode, chrono DateTime<FixedOffset/Utc> FromSql/ToSql, Arrow epoch conversion without double-adjustment, live matrix updates, public API baseline refresh, and PARITY_LEDGER intentional-divergence notes. Verified by targeted TSTZ/chrono/Arrow tests plus full release gates and semver-checks.

title: [parity] #376 thin TPC default-timeout (ORA-24756) inherited: live regression test + tracking (HOLD)
description:
Upstream python-oracledb #376 (thin, OPEN): a two-phase (TPC/XA) transaction begun + prepared on connection A and committed from connection B intermittently fails with ORA-24756 "transaction ... does not exist" (worse with any delay). init_oracle_client (thick) makes it disappear.

VERIFIED CURRENT STATE: REPRODUCE BY CONSTRUCTION. Our thin tpc_begin sends timeout=0 + flags=NEW byte-identical to upstream thin (sessionless.rs:288 writes the ub4 timeout; lib.rs:3010-3030 passes it through; default 0 comes from the Python wrapper, reference connection.py:1543). Upstream thin and thick send the SAME timeout=0/flags=NEW; the divergence is server-side interpretation of the thin TTC TPC_TXN_START path vs OCI OCITransStart -- so a faithful clone lands on the thin (failing) side. The change-state path always writes timeout 0 (sessionless.rs:334). No golden or live test covers the cross-connection+delay shape (reference test_4400_tpc.py is single-connection).

DISPOSITION: HOLD FOR PARITY. Do NOT unilaterally change the default timeout -- that would break byte-parity with the reference and could fail the conformance suite. Gate any behavioral change on upstream's resolution; if/when we choose to deviate, record it in docs/PARITY_LEDGER.md (intentional-divergence register).

WORK (test + tracking only, no behavior change now):
- Add a live cross-connection regression test that exercises the exact failure shape and (a) confirms we currently reproduce ORA-24756 (documents the inherited behavior), (b) becomes the parity check that flips to expecting success once upstream lands a fix and we adopt it.

TEST PLAN (testing-real-service-e2e-no-mocks):
- #[ignore] live test, two REAL connections to the 23ai container: on conn A xid=connection.xid(...); tpc_begin(xid); INSERT; tpc_prepare(); close A. Optionally sleep(configurable). On conn B tpc_recover() asserts xid present; tpc_commit(xid). Loop N iterations (the upstream repro fails after ~10-30). Assert + log the outcome with structured JSON-line phase/timing. Encode the current expectation as "reproduces ORA-24756 under delay" (XFAIL-style), clearly commented so a future fix flips it. No mocks -- real DB, real two-connection XA.
ACCEPTANCE: live test exists and deterministically exhibits the inherited behavior with logging; PARITY_LEDGER.md row kept current; default timeout unchanged.
close_reason: Completed as HOLD/tracking for 0.5.1: behavior remains byte-parity with upstream thin, but an ignored live two-connection TPC regression now documents the inherited ORA-24756 path and PARITY_LEDGER records the disposition. No runtime behavior change intended.

title: [parity] Immunity regression tests: lock in our wins over upstream (#595/#400/#596/#422)
description:
Lock in -- with explicit guard tests -- the four places the 2026-06-29 audit found us IMMUNE or BETTER than python-oracledb thin, so a future refactor cannot silently regress an advantage into bug-parity. These are advantages, not bugs; the work is purely test + a PARITY_LEDGER.md cross-link.

Targets (see docs/PARITY_LEDGER.md section 3):

1) #595 "unknown encoding" crash immunity. We keep no charsetid->codec table; we force AL32UTF8 (csid 873) in the define metadata and decode by csfrm only (codecs.rs:724-738), so a column with charsetid 0 / unmapped id cannot crash. TEST (testing-golden-artifacts + a metamorphic equivalence MR): parse_column_metadata + decode_text_value fed an IMPLICIT VARCHAR2 whose describe carries charset_id=0 -> assert csfrm kept, id ignored, bytes decode as UTF-8 with no error. MR: the column charsetid value does not affect the decoded string (vary id across {0, 1, 873, 0xFFFF}; output invariant). Bad bytes -> clean TtcDecode error, never a panic/LookupError.

2) #400 year -4712 (BCE) read advantage. chrono handles negative years (sql_convert.rs:368/411) where Python datetime cannot. TEST: unit decode of the wire bytes for year -4712 -> NaiveDate(-4712,1,1); golden decode artifact; #[ignore] live SELECT TO_DATE('-4712-01-01','SYYYY-MM-DD') returns it without error. Document the Oracle signed-year vs chrono astronomical-year 1-year labeling nuance. Note: encode still rejects year<1 (codecs.rs:13) -- assert that boundary explicitly so it is intentional, not accidental.

3) #596 Arrow TSTZ single-offset immunity. Arrow reuses the single-decoded row QueryValue (fetch.rs:1100/1273; arrow/builders.rs:228-248,343-363). TEST (metamorphic equivalence MR): for a TSTZ/LTZ column, Arrow timestamp value == row-path NaiveDateTime value, across random instants -- guards against any future Arrow path re-applying the offset.

4) #422 GROUP BY named-bind advantage. Our order_named_binds dedups by name so Oracle binds repeated placeholders by name and the optimizer matches expressions. Existing live test tests/repro_repeated_named_bind.rs (bead qp0) covers `select :v + :v`=10. TEST: ADD the positional-duplication counterpart -- a #[ignore] live test asserting the exact #422 query with POSITIONAL binds (user supplies the value per occurrence) reproduces ORA-00979, documenting that we match thin only on the positional path while the named path (the common case) avoids it.

ACCEPTANCE: each advantage has a regression guard (unit/golden/metamorphic where DB-free, #[ignore] live where it needs the server); mutation-validate the metamorphic MRs (planted offset/charset mutations are caught); PARITY_LEDGER.md section 3 cross-links each test. These are guards -- they must fail loudly if a refactor reintroduces a csid->codec lookup, a datetime min-year clamp, an Arrow double-offset, or per-occurrence named-bind expansion.
close_reason: Implemented for 0.5.1: added guard coverage for charset-id immunity (#595), BCE chrono date (#400), Arrow TSTZ epoch once (#596), and repeated named GROUP BY CASE bind dedup (#422), with PARITY_LEDGER cross-links. Verified by the focused tests and release gates.

title: live tests hardcode free23 schema and fixtures not portable
close_reason: RESOLVED — the portability concern is met and now documented. Verified: ALL live suites are PYO_TEST_* env-driven (live_transport_failover uses its own env(name,default) helper; F1 uses a synthetic TEST-NET black-hole address by design, not a lane). The hardcoded 'free23' strings are only DEFAULT FALLBACKS for a bare cargo test; the version_matrix overrides them per lane and PROVES portability (all live suites GREEN on xe18/xe21/free23 — see docs/VERSION_MATRIX.md result matrix). Added a 'Running one live suite directly' section to VERSION_MATRIX.md documenting the per-lane PYO_TEST_* coordinates (the real gap — how to run an individual suite by hand). The remaining residue is cosmetic: the env-fallback default is duplicated across ~15 files — tracked as optional P3 dedup rust-oracledb-live-test-conn-helper-8eew (NOT done: low value, 15-file churn risk).
