title: High-level PL/SQL routine-call API: RoutineCall/RoutineBind for OUT, IN OUT, function-return binds (GH #13)
description:
From oraclemcp/plsql-mcp via GitHub issue #13 (opened 2026-06-28). Consumer path: plsql-mcp -> oraclemcp-db -> oracledb. Origin bead oracle-plsql-converge-0lnu.17; plan note docs/plans/2026-06-19-plsql-mcp-convergence-and-1.0-release.md item 28 (upstream the driver gaps plsql-mcp needs, incl. OUT/IN-OUT bind ergonomics).

DEFERRED to 0.5.2 (decision 2026-06-29): this is a NEW ergonomic public API, not a typed-capability fix, so it is out of the small 0.5.1 capability-honesty patch. Filed now so it is tracked.

WHY: plsql-bindgen emits generated Rust wrappers for PL/SQL routines against a driver-neutral executor contract -- RoutineArg::In(BindValue) / Out / InOut(BindValue), and OracleExecutor::call_routine(plsql, args) -> Vec<BindValue> returning every OUT / IN OUT / function-return slot in bind order. Generated blocks look like `BEGIN :1 := hr.pkg.f(:2, :3); END;` and `BEGIN hr.pkg.p(:1, :2); END;`. Connection::execute_raw can ALREADY carry output bind shapes, so this is a canonical ergonomic helper over existing protocol, not new wire work -- every adapter currently rebuilds the same fragile mapping by hand.

REQUESTED SHAPE (naming open):
  enum RoutineBind { In(BindValue), Out{ oracle_type: OracleTypeHint }, InOut{ value: BindValue, oracle_type: OracleTypeHint }, Return{ oracle_type: OracleTypeHint } }
  struct RoutineCall { plsql, binds: Vec<RoutineBind>, timeout_ms: Option<u32> }
  Connection::call_routine(RoutineCall) -> Vec<BindValue> (OUT/IN-OUT/return slots in bind order), plus a BlockingConnection mirror.

WHAT THE HELPER MUST ENCAPSULATE (the 5 steps adapters redo today):
1. Convert a routine contract into driver BindValue::{Output, ReturnOutput, ...} rows.
2. Preserve function-return vs ordinary OUT vs IN OUT ordering.
3. Execute one anonymous block with timeout + cancellation semantics.
4. Project OutBinds / QueryResult back into typed scalar values.
5. Fail with typed, actionable errors when a value kind is unsupported.

ACCEPTANCE: additive public API (patch/minor-safe); preserves slot ordering exactly; honors Execute::timeout + cancel-on-drop; typed error for unsupported OracleTypeHint kinds; unit tests for ordering + projection + error cases; an #[ignore] live test calling a real package proc/function (IN, OUT, IN OUT, function return). Coordinate final naming with the plsql-mcp consumer before freezing.
close_reason: VERIFIED DONE — the high-level PL/SQL routine-call API shipped: RoutineCall (routine.rs:124), OutType enum (43), arg_out (163), arg_in_out (175, IN OUT), Connection::call_routine (297) + blocking wrapper. OUT / IN-OUT / function-return binds all present (GH #13 need met). Consumer note: plsql-mcp is retired, so the downstream wiring is moot regardless.

title: Pre-23ai Oracle server connect support (RESEND + classic auth flow)
description:
Field test 2026-07-03 (fresh install of the downstream MCP server, then doctor --online): every connect to a customer fleet (Oracle 19c, plain TCP, no encryption, no wallet) fails fast with "unknown TTC message type 11 at position 4", while python-oracledb 4.0.1 THIN connects to the same endpoints. Reproduced deterministically in the lab: gvenzl/oracle-xe:18-slim and gvenzl/oracle-xe:21-slim both fail identically with oracledb 0.5.1; python thin connects to both. 23ai (oracle-free:23) has always worked - it is the only version the live suite ever ran against.

Two-layer root cause (wire-confirmed with packet dumps):
1. RESEND: pre-23ai servers answer a CONNECT whose connect-data is long with an 8-byte TNS RESEND packet (00 08 00 00 0B 00 00 00; type 0x0B=11 at header offset 4). The accept-read only handles ACCEPT/REDIRECT/REFUSE and errors out, mislabeling the packet type as a TTC message type.
2. Fast-auth-only connect: after ACCEPT, the driver requires accept_info.supports_fast_auth (a 23ai-generation accept flag) and returns Error::FastAuthRequired otherwise. The classic protocol-negotiation + data-types + two-phase auth flow was a deliberate 0.5.x scope cut, so 18c/19c/21c servers are unreachable by design.

Deliverable: oracledb 0.5.2 connects to Oracle 18c/21c XE in the lab (and by extension the 19c field fleet), with regression tests, a standing multi-version live matrix, and honest connect diagnostics. Child beads carry the detail.
close_reason: All children closed: .1 RESEND, .2 classic session establishment, .3 packet-type diagnostics, .4 handshake goldens, .5 standing version matrix, .6 REDIRECT, .7 query-path token gating + framing. Pre-23ai (18c/19c/21c) connect + query support shipped in oracledb 0.6.0/0.7.0/0.7.1; four-lane live matrix (xe11 refusal + xe18/xe21/free23) green in CI and gating releases; ground-truth differential vs python-oracledb field-by-field identical.

title: Connect: resend CONNECT on TNS RESEND packet instead of failing
description:
Handle the TNS RESEND packet (type 11, 0x0B) during connect establishment.

Background: when the CONNECT packet's connect-data is long (descriptor split into CONNECT header + separate DATA packet), pre-23ai servers reply with an 8-byte RESEND packet: 00 08 00 00 0B 00 00 00. Wire-confirmed against gvenzl/oracle-xe:18-slim: python-oracledb (PYO_DEBUG_PACKETS=1) receives RESEND as its op-3 packet, resends the connect message (CONNECT + DATA descriptor packets), then receives ACCEPT and proceeds. Reference: python-oracledb protocol.pyx _process_single_message - "if message.resend: process again" (exactly one resend per attempt).

Current behavior: the accept-read match in crates/oracledb/src/lib.rs (~lines 2229-2246) handles ACCEPT, REDIRECT (RedirectUnsupported), REFUSE, and errors on everything else - so RESEND is fatal and every affected connect dies before authentication.

Fix: on RESEND, re-send the CONNECT packet (and the DATA packet carrying the descriptor when it was sent separately) and read the next packet; bound the retry (one resend, matching reference; a second RESEND is an error). Applies to both the plain-TCP and TLS paths.

Tests: sans-I/O unit test feeding the captured 8-byte RESEND bytes through the connect state handling; live test against XE 18/21 (which currently die here). NOTE: this fix alone does not produce a working pre-23ai connect - the flow then hits the fast-auth gate (sibling bead).
close_reason: Fixed and live-verified. Connect loop now: (a) sends connect data >230 bytes in a separate DATA packet after the CONNECT packet (reference messages/connect.pyx); (b) on a TNS RESEND packet (type 11) resends the CONNECT (+DATA) and reads again, bounded at 8 rounds (Error::ConnectResendLoop); (c) unexpected packet types get a named error (Error::UnexpectedConnectPacket via tns_packet_type_name) instead of the misleading UnknownMessageType{position:4}. Live: XE 18 and XE 21 both send RESEND on EVERY connect (even inline-size data) and now proceed to ACCEPT; 23ai unaffected (full smoke green). Wire-byte sans-I/O RESEND fixture still tracked in sibling .4.

title: Connect: classic (non-fast-auth) session establishment for pre-23ai servers
description:
Implement the classic (non-fast-auth) TTC session establishment used by every Oracle server generation before 23ai.

Background: crates/oracledb/src/lib.rs (~2248) hard-requires accept_info.supports_fast_auth (accept flag TNS_ACCEPT_FLAG_FAST_AUTH, parsed in oracledb-protocol thin/connect.rs) and returns Error::FastAuthRequired otherwise. Fast auth bundles protocol negotiation + data types + auth phase one into one round trip and exists only on 23ai-generation servers. Result: 18c/19c/21c are unreachable by design. Field-blocking (customer fleet is Oracle 19c); reproduces locally on gvenzl/oracle-xe:18-slim and :21-slim while python-oracledb 4.0.1 thin connects fine.

Implement, gated on supports_fast_auth == false (reference: python-oracledb impl/thin/protocol.pyx connect phase two + messages protocol.pyx / data_types.pyx / auth.pyx):
1. TTC protocol-negotiation message (msg type 1); parse server capabilities from response.
2. Data-types message (msg type 2); parse/skip response.
3. Auth phase one function call; parse AUTH_VFR_DATA + session data.
4. Auth phase two with password verifier; parse final auth response; verify server response MAC via combo key (existing crypto path).

Reuse notes: response parsers already exist (skip_protocol_message, skip_data_types_response, parse_auth_response_with_limits); the fast-auth payload builder already embeds protocol + data-types writers - extract them as standalone message builders rather than duplicating. Capability care: END_OF_RESPONSE is only present when negotiated (23ai); the auth response parse loop must terminate correctly for older servers without it. Token auth (access_token) remains TCPS + fast-auth only; classic flow is password/verifier auth.

Acceptance: BlockingConnection::connect plus a round-trip query (select 7+5 from dual; v$version banner) green against XE 18 and XE 21; 23ai fast-auth path byte-identical (existing goldens stay green); full workspace gates pass. This bead is the release driver for oracledb 0.5.2.
notes: PROGRESS 2026-07-03: session establishment itself is DONE and live-verified - XE 18 and XE 21 authenticate (session_id/serial returned); 23ai fast-auth path regression-free (full smoke + workspace tests green). Landed: classic protocol-negotiation + data-types round trips as exact slices of the fast-auth prefix (build_protocol_negotiation_payload / build_data_types_payload, layout pinned by unit test); standalone auth phase one (build_auth_phase_one_payload); message-driven completion for classic connect responses (classic_connect_response_is_complete + read_classic_data_response); ub8 pipeline-token gating on ttc_field_version >= 23_1_EXT_1 for auth phase two + change password; field-version negotiation fixed from max() to min() per reference capabilities.pyx. Remaining before this bead's acceptance (round-trip query): the query path - see child bead pre23ai-query-path.
close_reason: Acceptance met: BlockingConnection::connect + round-trip queries green on XE 18 and XE 21 (classic protocol-negotiation/data-types/two-phase-auth as exact fast-auth-prefix slices; message-driven completion; ub8 gating; min() field-version negotiation); 23ai fast-auth path regression-free, goldens byte-identical; full workspace gates green. Query-path work landed via child bead .7.

title: Diagnostics: distinct error for unexpected TNS packet type (not a TTC message type)
description:
Unexpected TNS packet types are currently reported by reusing ProtocolError::UnknownMessageType with a hardcoded position 4 (crates/oracledb/src/lib.rs accept-read). That error text names TTC *message* types - an application-layer concept - while the byte in question is the network-layer *packet* type field at header offset 4. In the field this mislabel steered a full triage session toward TTC IO_VECTOR (message 11) and SDU-reassembly hypotheses; the actual packet was RESEND.

Fix: add a distinct error variant (e.g. UnexpectedPacketType { packet_type, phase }) that names known TNS packet types (CONNECT, ACCEPT, ACK, REFUSE, REDIRECT, DATA, NULL, ABORT, RESEND, MARKER, ATTENTION, CONTROL) and the connect phase in which it arrived. Error text should be self-triaging, e.g. "unexpected TNS packet type 11 (RESEND) while waiting for ACCEPT". Audit other sites that reuse UnknownMessageType for non-TTC bytes (dpl.rs has two raise sites - verify layer correctness there too).

Tests: unit tests assert the new message text for RESEND and an unknown type; grep-level check that no caller still constructs UnknownMessageType with a literal position.
notes: PROGRESS: connect-phase mislabel fixed - Error::UnexpectedPacket names the TNS packet type (tns_packet_type_name) and phase-agnostic wording; used by both the ACCEPT wait and the classic probed reader. REMAINING: audit the flag-framed boundary reader (read_data_response_boundary_seeded non-DATA arm) and dpl.rs sites that still reuse UnknownMessageType{position:4} for packet-layer bytes.
close_reason: Residue audit complete. Converted BOTH remaining driver sites that stuffed the NETWORK packet-type byte (header offset 4) into ProtocolError::UnknownMessageType{position:4}: (1) the flag-framed boundary reader non-DATA arm (read_data_response_boundary_seeded) and (2) the break-drain phase-A reader (drain_break_response_recovery_with_limits) — both now return Error::UnexpectedPacket(packet_type), whose text names TNS packet types ('unexpected TNS packet type 11 (RESEND)'). Audited every other UnknownMessageType raise site: dpl.rs (2 sites) already uses reader.position() on TTC message bytes inside DATA payloads (correct layer — no change needed), as do auth.rs (2), subscr.rs (3, incl. the position:0 site which is a genuine offset-0 TTC OAC byte), lob.rs (2), aq.rs (3), fetch.rs (2, with embedded-error fallback). No protocol-crate site ever sees a packet header byte (the crate is sans-I/O over payloads), so no new protocol-crate error variant was needed. Grep check: 'position: [0-9]' across all crates now matches only subscr.rs:379 (legit TTC offset 0) and an unrelated pyshim SODA cursor struct field. Tests: unexpected_packet_error_names_tns_packet_types (message text for RESEND + unknown 99), flag_framed_reader_labels_non_data_packet_as_packet_layer_error and break_drain_labels_non_data_packet_as_packet_layer_error (mock-listener per-site tests asserting Error::UnexpectedPacket + self-triaging text); the classic probed reader was already covered by classic_probed_read_rejects_non_data_packet from the earlier phase of this bead.

title: Sans-I/O wire regression tests for the pre-23ai handshake
description:
Pin the pre-23ai connect contract with sans-I/O wire-byte regression tests in oracledb-protocol, in the spirit of the existing golden tests (pipeline_golden.rs, dbobject_golden.rs).

Fixtures come from lab captures only (gvenzl XE 18/21 containers; python-oracledb 4.0.1 PYO_DEBUG_PACKETS dumps of the working handshake, plus our fixed driver's own exchanges once classic connect lands). Cover at minimum: the 8-byte RESEND reply; the pre-23ai ACCEPT payload (flags WITHOUT fast-auth); classic protocol-negotiation response; data-types response; auth phase one response (verifier data); auth phase two response (session data, no END_OF_RESPONSE terminator). Assert both successful parses and the terminate-without-END_OF_RESPONSE loop behavior.

Keep all fixtures free of anything environment-identifying: lab container service names (XEPDB1), lab users only. No customer data of any kind in fixtures, test names, or comments.
close_reason: Sans-I/O golden regression suite landed (oracledb-protocol/tests/pre23ai_handshake_golden.rs + tests/golden/*.hex, lab captures only): RESEND 8-byte packet, below-floor 11g ACCEPT (refusal precedes decode), XE18 classic ACCEPT without fast-auth flags, FREE23 contrast ACCEPT, classic protocol-negotiation/data-types/auth phase one+two responses proven complete ONLY at their terminal message incl. incomplete-prefix read-more behavior. Bonus fixes with it: below-floor server refusal implemented (UnsupportedVersion{version,minimum}); classic connect-phase reads handle break MARKERs so wrong-password surfaces ORA-01017.

title: Standing multi-version live matrix: XE 18 + XE 21 + FREE 23ai
description:
The 0.5.x line was only ever live-tested against gvenzl/oracle-free:23 - which is exactly why a connect path that cannot reach any pre-23ai server shipped and was first noticed in a customer field install. Make server-version coverage a standing gate.

Scope: scripted matrix of gvenzl/oracle-xe:18-slim, gvenzl/oracle-xe:21-slim, gvenzl/oracle-free:23-slim on distinct host ports (extend scripts/container.sh or add a compose file); run the live smoke + conformance-relevant suite against each; document in README testing section and wire into the release checklist so no release ships without pre-23ai coverage. Genuine 19c (Oracle Container Registry EE image, requires OTN account login, free for dev/test) documented as an optional extra lane, not required for the gate.

Note: 18c and 21c bracket 19c on the relevant protocol behaviors (RESEND on long connect-data, no fast-auth, no END_OF_RESPONSE); both reproduce the field failure byte-identically.
notes: SCOPE RAISED (operator directive 2026-07-04): smoke is NOT enough. Root lesson - the thin driver was fast-auth-only from its FIRST connect milestone (6378c40) and was only ever validated against 23ai; the thick-to-thin port silently narrowed the server-version envelope and nothing guarded it. Required standing gate: (1) version_matrix.sh gains a 'full' subcommand per lane: connect, multi-packet fetch with result verification, bind DML + rollback + commit, LOB read/write, describe/metadata, error paths (bad SQL, bad credentials) - assertions on VALUES not just exit codes; (2) release_preflight/checklist must require a fresh full-matrix pass recorded for the release SHA - a release CANNOT ship without pre-23ai lanes green; (3) CI wiring: 23ai lane on every PR, full XE18+XE21+23ai matrix on release branches + nightly (gvenzl images as GH Actions services); (4) genuine 19c EE lane (Oracle Container Registry, OTN login) documented as an operator-run pre-release step.
close_reason: Standing gate landed: version_matrix.sh full (deep value-asserting per-lane suite via examples/matrix_full.rs: session identity, 600-row multi-packet fetch verified value-by-value, wide rows above one SDU, bind DML rows_affected, rollback + cross-connection commit visibility, CLOB/BLOB above one chunk, describe/metadata, NULLs, type round-trips, error paths); new xe11 below-floor lane asserts the structured protocol-version refusal (314 vs floor 315, reference DPY-3010 parity); scripts/release_matrix_gate.sh writes per-SHA verdicts and release_preflight.sh refuses a release tag without a committed all-green matrix artifact; .github/workflows/version-matrix.yml runs the full matrix on main pushes touching crates plus nightly. Verified live: all four lanes FULL GREEN.

title: Connect: implement listener REDIRECT handling
description:
Error::RedirectUnsupported is returned when the listener answers CONNECT with a REDIRECT packet. Shared-server, RAC, and some listener configs redirect routinely - on pre-23ai stacks this is the next protocol gap in the exact code path the classic-connect work touches.

Reference: python-oracledb ConnectMessage redirect handling - read redirect data (host:port + new connect data), reconnect the transport to the redirected address, resend the connect message there.

Acceptance: unit test with a synthetic REDIRECT payload; live verification if a redirecting listener is available in the lab (optional). Depends on classic connect landing first so the redirected session can actually authenticate on older servers.
close_reason: Implemented listener REDIRECT handling in the connect loop (crates/oracledb/src/lib.rs): on REDIRECT the driver assembles the redirect data (u16be length prefix, continuation across follow-up REDIRECT/DATA packets), splits '<address>\0<connect data>' (reference protocol.pyx _connect_phase_one), reconnects the transport to the redirected host:port via a shared dial closure (TLS params resolved once and reused for tcps originals), and resends CONNECT there with TNS_PACKET_FLAG_REDIRECT (new protocol const) and the redirect-supplied connect data; RESEND/REDIRECT interleave handled (resend counter resets per hop, redirect flag persists across resends); redirect chains bounded at 8 hops (Error::ConnectRedirectLoop). Error::RedirectUnsupported now kept ONLY for transport-protocol changes (tcps->tcp downgrade refused, incl. fail-closed when the redirect omits PROTOCOL on a tcps original — documented deviation from reference which ignores redirect protocol; tcp->tcps upgrade also refused). New Error::InvalidRedirectData for malformed shapes (truncated length, missing NUL, unparseable/hostless address, non-UTF8). Tests: 3 sans-I/O unit tests (payload prefix well-formed/partial/len-only/trailing/truncated; target parse well-formed/missing-NUL/no-HOST/garbage; protocol-change refusal matrix) + 3 real-socket mock-listener tests (full redirect hop asserting flag 0x04 + redirect connect data on the second listener, RESEND-before-redirect + chunked redirect data + RESEND-after-redirect keeps flag, self-redirect loop bounded with structured ConnectRedirectLoop(9) and all 9 hops observed). The transport reconnect proved unit-testable with two real TcpListeners — no seam-only fallback needed. Live: xe18 (RESEND lane) + free23 smoke green after the connect-loop rework; not live-verified against a real redirecting listener (none in lab, as anticipated by the bead).

title: Pre-23ai query path: gate ub8 function headers + message-driven response completion
description:
Discovered while landing the classic connect flow (parent bead): connect + full password auth is now GREEN on XE 18/21, but the first query then fails - the QUERY PATH still assumes 23ai conventions in two ways:

1. Function-message headers: build_function_payload_with_seq_and_token and the execute/fetch/lob/commit builders write the ub8 pipeline-token unconditionally; reference messages/base.pyx _write_function_code writes it only when caps.ttc_field_version >= TNS_CCAP_FIELD_VERSION_23_1_EXT_1 (18). Pre-23ai servers parse the stray byte(s) as message content. Observed live: ORA-03120 (two-task conversion integer overflow) on XE 21 for "select 7+5 from dual". The same gating fix applied to auth phase two / change password in the parent work must be spread across every function builder, keyed off the connection's negotiated capabilities.

2. Response completion: read_data_response_boundary decides end-of-response by the END_OF_RESPONSE/EOF DATA flags - 23ai request-boundary framing that pre-23ai servers (protocol version < 319) never send. Observed live: XE 18 query hangs until the call timeout. The connect phase now uses a message-walking completion checker (classic_connect_response_is_complete), but execute/fetch responses cannot be walked content-blind (row data needs column metadata), so completion must be integrated into the response parsing itself: pre-23ai responses terminate at their terminal message - the ERROR message (msg 4) after rows/status per reference base.pyx/withdata.pyx processing, STATUS (msg 9) for plain calls. Suggested seam: thread supports_end_of_response (already parsed from ACCEPT in AcceptInfo) into the fetch/execute parse layer and let the parser report both consumed-bytes and response-complete, replacing the flag-only decision when the capability is absent.

Acceptance: smoke example fully green (select 7+5, typed query, clean logoff) on XE 18 and XE 21; 23ai conformance/golden suites unchanged; live matrix (sibling .5) green end to end.
close_reason: Done, live-verified on XE 18 + XE 21 + 23ai FREE. (a) ub8 pipeline-token gated on negotiated ttc_field_version >= 23_1_EXT_1 via centralized TtcWriter::write_function_header/write_piggyback_header; execute/fetch/lob/aq/subscr/plain-function builders migrated; sessionless left as-is (23ai-only feature). (b) Probe-driven classic response framing: ConnectionCore::read_data_response_probed (+ flushing-out-binds variant) accumulates DATA packets and completes when the site's own parser stops reporting decode shortfall; converted execute, fetch (owned+borrowed), commit/rollback/ping/logoff, LOB read/write/trim/temp, change_password, scroll_cursor; pipelining fails closed on classic connections. Bonus: fixed pre-existing duplicate-row fetch-continuation bug in rows.rs (Rows::collect drained batch before next_batch could seed dup-compression; reproduced at HEAD on 23ai). Matrix smoke green all lanes; 500-row + wide multi-packet fetch + DML/commit verified per lane; full gates green; 23ai goldens byte-identical. Still flag-framed (future coverage): sessionless, TPC, AQ, subscriptions, direct-path, dbobject metadata fetches - guarded or 23ai-gated.

title: [bug] DbObject long (>252B) attribute value decode reads u32be instead of chunked ub4
description: E8 R2 Pass D. dbobject.rs writer image_write_value_bytes(:159) emits TNS_LONG form as chunked image_write_ub4 segments + 0 terminator; reader read_value_bytes(:88) read a single fixed u32be -> mis-decode of DbObject attrs >252B on fetch. FIXED: reader loops read_ub4 chunks (matches python-oracledb v4.0.1 packet.pyx). Round-trip regression at 252/253/32767/32768.
close_reason: CORRECTED. The Round-2 fix (commit 9ffc785) matched the WRONG (chunked ub4 252-cutoff) format on both sides. The correct DbObject image format is SINGLE big-endian u32, 245-cutoff, no chunking (verified vs reference dbobject.pyx write_length/read_length + constants.pxi:230 TNS_OBJ_MAX_SHORT_LENGTH=245). R3 FIX 5 reverts the chunked reader + fixes the writer to single-u32be. Round-trip regression at 244/245/246/65535/70000. Conformance 0 regressions.

title: Implement real temporary CLOB input bind support for 32K XMLTYPE inserts

title: Typed thin AuthMode capability surface + Kerberos & RADIUS/native-MFA (oraclemcp #3/#4)
description: From oraclemcp GH issues #3 (Kerberos) and #4 (RADIUS/native MFA). VERIFIED current state: ConnectOptions (lib.rs:700-742) exposes only user/password/proxy_user — no way to express Kerberos (keytab/principal/constrained-delegation) or RADIUS/native-MFA (challenge) auth. oraclemcp fails-closed today with an explicit unsupported-auth diagnostic. ASK (per both issues): either real thin-mode support OR a TYPED capability/error that says the mode is unsupported by this driver/protocol build. The UNIFYING need across #1-#4: a typed AuthMode surface so a caller can (a) select a mode and (b) receive a machine-classifiable 'unsupported' result distinguishable from bad-creds / missing-service / TLS-failure / listener-failure (so 'oraclemcp doctor' is precise). SUGGESTED SHAPE: an AuthMode enum (Password/Proxy/External/IamToken/Kerberos/Radius/...) with thin support where feasible + a typed UnsupportedAuthMode error otherwise. REQS: redact keytab/principal/token/challenge from all formatted output; unit tests for construction+redaction+failure-classification; live tests gated behind real Kerberos/RADIUS infra. RELATES: 5bh (IAM token, #1), o0b (external/passwordless, #2).
acceptance_criteria: 0.5.1 deliverable (TYPED SURFACE ONLY): an AuthMode enum (Password/Proxy/External/IamToken/Kerberos/Radius/...) + a typed UnsupportedAuthMode error, so a caller selects a mode and receives a machine-classifiable "unsupported by this thin build" result distinct from bad-creds / missing-service / TLS-failure / listener-failure. Unit tests for construction + redaction + failure-classification. Redact keytab/principal/token/challenge in ALL formatted/Debug output. The REAL Kerberos and RADIUS/MFA backends are split to the deferred follow-up bead and are explicitly NOT in 0.5.1. Shares the AuthMode spine with rust-oracledb-o0b. Scoped into Release 0.5.1. GH #3/#4.
notes: Real backends split to rust-oracledb-kerberos-radius-backends-bpsh (deferred). 0.5.1 ships only the typed AuthMode + UnsupportedAuthMode surface.
close_reason: Implemented typed AuthMode/AuthCapabilities/UnsupportedAuthMode for password, proxy, external, IAM token, Kerberos, and RADIUS; unsupported thin modes fail before network I/O; tests cover construction, classification, and redaction.

title: Investigate repeated named-bind under-binding vs python-oracledb per-occurrence (adjacent to #422)
description: While confirming we do NOT reproduce python-oracledb #422 (ORA-00979), the sweep flagged a divergence: python-oracledb thin's StatementParser adds a BindInfo per placeholder OCCURRENCE (statement.pyx) and binds positionally, so a name repeated N times sends N slots. Our crate (sql_convert.rs order_named_binds) emits each named value ONCE per unique name in first-appearance order. This avoids #422 but may under-bind when a :name repeats in the SQL. VERIFY: does the server accept once-per-name for repeated placeholders, or do we need per-occurrence expansion? Add a test: 'select :a, :a from dual' with named bind :a -> assert correct result / no ORA error. If broken, expand named binds per-occurrence.
close_reason: NOT A BUG (verified LIVE against Oracle Free 23ai). Hypothesis: order_named_binds (once per distinct name) under-binds repeated SQL placeholders vs python-oracledb thin's per-occurrence (statement.pyx:343). DISPROVEN: live repro query_named('select :v + :v from dual', :v=5) returns 10, not ORA-01008 — Oracle binds the repeated placeholder BY NAME, so once-per-distinct-name is correct AND sidesteps #422 (per-occurrence optimizer confusion). Added regression test crates/oracledb/tests/repro_repeated_named_bind.rs.

title: [bug] number_to_json silently loses precision (lossy f64 vs stated no-loss intent)
description: E8 R5. sql_convert.rs:512 tries i64/u64/f64; accepts any finite f64 -> high-precision NUMBER lossy despite 'preserve exact text' comment (string fallback never reached for finite). FIX: f64 only when lossless, else Value::String. Verified.
close_reason: FIXED. number_to_json keeps exact text when f64 is lossy (no-loss intent honored); exact/small values unchanged. Regression added.

title: FromSql trait: typed extraction (i64/String/chrono/uuid/serde_json/Vec<f32>/Decimal), feature-gated
close_reason: Merged in bp-dx (commit e0e3134)

title: Built-in retry executor over the ORA taxonomy with explicit idempotency gating
notes: Dueling-wizards (555). Leverages shipped TRANSIENT/CONNECTION_LOST taxonomy + is_retryable/is_transient/is_connection_lost. CRITICAL differentiator both reviewers flag: a committed-but-connection-lost DML can DOUBLE-APPLY, so auto-retry MUST gate on caller-asserted idempotency and default to NOT retrying non-SELECT on connection loss (else footgun). Circuit-breaker optional/separate. Effort M.

title: Differential fuzz oracle: same wire bytes -> rust decoder vs python-oracledb decoder, assert identical
notes: Dueling-wizards BLIND SPOT (neither wizard proposed; judge rated high-yield). Feed identical wire bytes to rust-oracledb parse_query_response AND python-oracledb's thin decoder (pyshim already links it); assert identical decoded values. Highest-confidence parity proof available; catches SILENT value-divergence — exactly the bug class the 8 shipped fixes came from — that no-panic fuzzing + example tests structurally miss. Extends the existing 9-target fuzz harness. Effort M.
close_reason: Differential oracle landed (container round-trip, partial fidelity stated honestly): 5,944 cases, 0 divergences, negative-control-verified. Commit f977a2f.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: DONE (partial-fidelity differential, stated honestly). Built a CONTAINER ROUND-TRIP differential (harness/differential/diff_oracle.py): server encodes, both rust-oracledb (via oracledb_pyshim thin_impl swap) and reference python-oracledb thin decode the SAME wire bytes; fetched Python values asserted identical. NUMBER compared as exact decimal string (not lossy float); DATE/TIMESTAMP via datetime + TO_CHAR FF9. Covered NUMBER + DATE/TIMESTAMP (highest-divergence-risk codecs). 3 seeds x 2000 cases = 5,944 compared, 0 divergences. Negative control proves comparator catches injected divergence. FIDELITY CAVEAT: weaker than a pure in-process decoder differential (only server-emitted well-formed payloads; shared server) — a pure differential is impractical because python-oracledb decoders are Cython cdef over C OracleDataBuffer*, not callable on raw bytes. Pure round-trip decode(encode(x))==x already covered in thin/proptests.rs; this adds the cross-engine dimension. Commit f977a2f.

title: Release 0.5.1 -- downstream capability-honesty + doc-truth patch
description:
First patch release on the new +0.0.1 cadence (0.5.0 -> 0.5.1). Theme: ship the SMALL, downstream-unblocking half of the open oraclemcp issues -- typed, machine-classifiable capability surfaces and accurate docs -- WITHOUT the heavy auth/wallet backends. Additive-only, so it stays patch-safe under the BLOCKING cargo-semver-checks gate. It directly feeds the oraclemcp soak that gates 1.0 [rust-oracledb-vm2f]: a precise `oraclemcp doctor` needs exactly these typed surfaces, so shipping them accelerates the 1.0 path while the soak runs.

IN SCOPE (blockers of this release):
- rust-oracledb-o0b : typed passwordless/external AuthMode intent with no dummy creds + capability metadata. GH #2.
- rust-oracledb-qm4 : typed AuthMode enum + UnsupportedAuthMode error for Kerberos and RADIUS/native-MFA. GH #3/#4. Real backends are split to a deferred spinout.
- rust-oracledb-x1p : docs/SUPPORT.md wallet-format matrix + typed format diagnostics + redaction sweep. GH #6. Encrypted PEM / p12 split to a deferred spinout.
- doc-truth refresh chore: README fuzz-target count 10 -> 20; refresh stale 2236 -> 2462 in RELEASE_CERTIFICATION / FAKE_PARITY_AUDIT / GROUND_TRUTH.
- rust-oracledb-sqp2 : rustdoc broken-link warnings from the public-api snapshots.
- SUPPORT.md known-limitation note for clvm F1: DSN transport_connect_timeout is currently silently ignored (footgun) -- document it.

EXPLICITLY DEFERRED (NOT this release):
- Real Kerberos and RADIUS/MFA auth backends -- spinout from qm4.
- Encrypted ewallet.pem PKCS8 decrypt + standalone ewallet.p12 -- spinout from x1p, target 0.5.2.
- High-level PL/SQL routine-call API, GH #13 -- new bead, target 0.5.2.
- IAM request-signing [cco], transport-HA epic [clvm F2/F3/F4], epic-57z differentiators -- post-1.0.

RELEASE CUT / ACCEPTANCE CHECKLIST:
- Bump workspace version 0.5.0 -> 0.5.1 in Cargo.toml; bump inter-crate pins; CHANGELOG entry with Added/Fixed only, zero Breaking.
- Gates green: cargo fmt --check; cargo clippy --workspace --no-deps -D warnings; cargo test --workspace; cargo test --features cassette; cargo semver-checks must classify the diff as patch-safe vs the 0.5.0 baseline; cargo deny.
- Re-run python-oracledb conformance harness/run.sh baseline|rust|diff -> 0 regressions vs the recorded 2462 passed / 116 skipped baseline.
- Re-run the live lane on a CLEAN container to avoid the n5s2 stale-CQN env failure.
- release_preflight.sh version-pin guard + standalone .crate build; regenerate SBOM/provenance at 0.5.1.
- Redaction check: no keytab/principal/token/challenge/wallet-path/password in any formatted error or Debug output across the new surfaces.
- Publish to crates.io; tag; notify oraclemcp that 0.5.1 is available so the doctor-precision work and the soak use it.

title: [ROAD] rust-oracledb → 1.0: correctness-hardened, contract-stable thin Oracle driver
description:
# Road to 1.0 — program epic

**North Star:** ship a correctness-hardened, diagnosable, resource-bounded pure-Rust async thin-mode Oracle driver. `oraclemcp` is the primary consumer (ships as a single static binary), but the crate is publicly usable and carries a FULL SemVer + support contract. 1.0 is a maturity AND compatibility milestone.

Umbrella for the whole program, split into 5 waves (child epics): W0 Evidence & CI · W1 Architecture · W2 0.3.0 migration · W3 Qualification · W4 Freeze & release. Source plan: docs/ROAD_TO_1_0.md (+ docs/API_DESIGN.md for the W1-T3 public API). These beads are meant to be self-contained — you should not need the markdown.

## Binding decisions (ADRs; files written by W0-T1)
- ADR-0001 — Keep asupersync + the pinned nightly (nightly-2026-05-11) through 1.0. oracledb is oraclemcp's engine; oraclemcp ships one static binary, so nightly is build-time-only and invisible to its users. asupersync is an asset (cancel-correct; LabRuntime+DPOR reach a bug class the single-threaded python suite cannot). Review triggers: try_trait_v2 stabilizes; an equivalent stable path appears; the pin blocks a security update; external Rust-crate adoption becomes a product goal.
- ADR-0002 — Full external SemVer + support contract; blocking cargo-semver-checks from 0.3.0 (decision (b)). Breaking cleanup happens in a 0.3.0 migration release. semver-checks runs ADVISORY during 0.3.0 dev (so the redesign isn't blocked) and flips to BLOCKING the moment 0.3.0 ships (0.3.0 = baseline), staying blocking. The gate guards UNINTENDED breaks only; intentional breaks are allowed but take the correct bump (0.x: a break is a MINOR bump, 0.3.x→0.4.0; patch must be non-breaking) + a baseline refresh in the same release. Rationale: a driver is a bounded domain — once parity+extras land and the API is designed deliberately pre-0.3.0, growth is overwhelmingly additive (#[non_exhaustive] value enums + as_* accessors; new methods); Oracle's backward-compatible TTC/TNS never forces an API break.
- ADR-0003 — Release evidence is per exact candidate SHA. Scheduled canary/soak lanes run `main` for DISCOVERY; the 1.0 gate is an explicit exact-SHA release-qualification run on the frozen RC, executed by the agent/operator and judged by objective evidence gates rather than subjective approval.

## Cross-cutting resolved decisions
- The connection pool is ASYNC-NATIVE with a `block_on` sync facade (mirrors the Connection/BlockingConnection model); loom is DROPPED in favour of asupersync DPOR. The old Condvar/GIL pool design existed only so the PyO3 pyshim could release the GIL; the pyshim is publish=false and must not shape the shipped driver. See W1-T7 + W3-E4.
- Five asupersync-leverage refinements are folded in, each verified against the vendored asupersync 0.3.4 source: single op-deadline (W1-T3), bounded recovery budget + checkpoint invariant (W1-T2), Outcome/CancelKind discipline (W1-T6), lab oracles-as-gates + crashpacks + chaos/VirtualTcp (W3-E3/E4).
- R10 RESOLVED: cargo-semver-checks 0.48.0 runs cleanly under the pin (196 checks each on oracledb-protocol + oracledb); gate those two LIBRARY crates only — the oracledb-derive proc-macro is skipped (guard it with trybuild). Async-bridge RESOLVED: no native PyO3↔asupersync bridge exists (asupersync 0.3.4 exposes no foreign-loop/external-waker surface); the shim stays spawn_blocking; nto won't-fixed (W0-T4).

## Out of scope (explicit)
- Group-A auth/wallet: GH #2/#3/#4/#6 — Kerberos, RADIUS/native-MFA, passwordless-external, broader wallet (beads rust-oracledb-o0b/qm4/x1p).
- The `57z` "beat-python" enhancement epic (rust-oracledb-57z) and its differentiators — post-1.0, except a winnowed correctness set via W3-E10.

## Definition of done
1.0.0 published to crates.io (W4-T4) with: the coherent four-family API + retained low-level surface (nothing lost), typed errors + connection disposition, ProtocolLimits resource bounds, the async-native pool, a BLOCKING SemVer contract live since 0.3.0, docs/SUPPORT.md matrix, SBOM + build provenance/attestations, standalone-tested .crate artifacts, and the full Wave-3 correctness evidence bundle green on the exact RC SHA.

title: [WAVE-0] Evidence & CI — inventories, ADRs, tiered CI, feature/SemVer/stable lanes, API ledger
description:
Wave 0 stands up the evidence + CI substrate every later wave consumes. Nothing here ships product behavior; it makes later work measurable, regenerable, and gate-able.

Tasks: W0-T1 pin baseline + generate inventories + write the 3 ADR files (root of almost everything); W0-T2 tiered reusable CI (required/canary/soak/release-qualification) + docs/TOOLCHAIN.md re-pin runbook; W0-T3 feature-profile + SemVer + stable-protocol lanes (cargo-semver-checks wired ADVISORY now); W0-T4 close the `nto` bead off-gate (PM cleanup, not a 1.0 gate); W0-T5 API-surface ledger + accidental-leak adjudication (the driving artifact of the API audit).

Gate-relevant outputs (W0-T2 CI tiers, W0-T3 lanes, W0-T5 ledger) feed Wave 4's freeze (W4-T1) and the qualification run (W4-T2). W0-T1 is the prerequisite root for W0-T2/T3/T5 and, via the ledger, for the Wave-1 audit work.

Latest verification for the ledger substrate passed scripts/check_api_ledger.sh and br dep cycles --json.

title: [W0-T1] Pin baseline + generate inventories + write the 3 ADR files
description:
WHY: Every later count/claim in this program must be regenerable, not prose. This task pins the reviewed source commit and emits machine-generated baselines under docs/baseline/ so a future contributor can tell whether a number changed because the CODE changed or the PLAN went stale. It also creates the ADR files the whole roadmap references (docs/adr/) — nothing else creates them.

SCOPE:
1. Record the source commit; write a regenerable script (e.g. scripts/gen_baseline.sh) that produces docs/baseline/*.
2. Generate inventories: public-API listing per supported profile, enabled-feature matrix, fuzz-target manifest dump, test inventory, version/pin set.
3. Write docs/adr/0001-nightly-asupersync.md, 0002-semver-contract.md, 0003-exact-sha-evidence.md — each with the decision + its objective review triggers (verbatim from the program epic).

VERIFIED BASELINE NOTES (reviewed commit; these are notes, not acceptance): crates/oracledb/src/lib.rs has 159 pub fn, 66 pub async fn, 19 execute_query* variants (10 async + 9 blocking); 118 public structs/enums across the 3 published crates; 0 #[non_exhaustive].

ACCEPTANCE: docs/baseline/* and docs/adr/000{1,2,3}-*.md committed; the baseline is regenerable by running the script (re-run yields identical output on an unchanged tree).
TESTS: a CI/check step that re-runs the baseline script and fails if docs/baseline/ would change without a code change (drift guard).
DEPS: none (foundational root). Feeds W0-T2, W0-T3, W0-T5.

title: [W0-T2] Tiered, reusable CI (required/canary/soak/release-qualification) + TOOLCHAIN.md runbook
description:
WHY: CI today is only push/PR to main (no schedule/workflow_dispatch except release.yml). The program needs DISCOVERY lanes (find toolchain breaks + accumulate fuzz/property evidence on moving commits) separated from a single QUALIFYING gate (ADR-0003: exact-SHA). One reusable workflow keeps the four tiers running the SAME commands at different budgets so they can't drift.

SCOPE: a reusable _quality.yml (workflow_call; inputs: profile, budget, candidate_sha) invoked by four tiers — required.yml (PR/push deterministic checks = current gates), canary.yml (daily at a NON-ZERO minute; floating-nightly early-warning per ADR-0001 + moderate fuzz/property budgets), soak.yml (weekly deep, sharded fuzz/property/model/perf), release-qualification.yml (MANUAL, exact-SHA, full gate). Plus docs/TOOLCHAIN.md: the canary DETECTS a nightly break, this runbook is the RESPONSE (why nightly, how the pin is chosen, exact re-pin steps).

KEY CORRECTNESS FIXES (from review): evidence jobs FAIL normally — non-blocking is achieved via branch-protection SELECTION, NOT continue-on-error (which makes a job "pass" and defeats a later if:failure()). A separate if:always() reporter inspects needs.*.result, runs only on schedule/manual, and creates/updates ONE fingerprint-deduplicated issue. Default permissions: contents:read; grant issues:write/attestation only to reporter/release jobs; pin third-party actions to full SHAs; set timeout-minutes + concurrency; avoid cron at minute 0.

ACCEPTANCE: the four tiers run the same commands at different budgets; a forced evidence failure opens exactly ONE issue; required CI unchanged in scope; a scheduled red job does NOT silently pass; docs/TOOLCHAIN.md lets a fresh agent re-pin from it alone. Link TOOLCHAIN.md from README + the oracledb skill.
TESTS: a deliberately-failing evidence job in a test branch proves (a) it fails, (b) exactly one dedup issue opens, (c) it does not flip required CI.
DEPS: W0-T1. Feeds W4-T1 (freeze needs the CI tiers) + W4-T2 (uses release-qualification.yml with candidate_sha).
close_reason: Completed: W0-T2.1 reusable workflow, W0-T2.2 tier callers, W0-T2.3 reporter/security hardening, and W0-T2.4 TOOLCHAIN runbook are closed and pushed. Live verification: forced canary run 27847058764 failed the evidence lane and succeeded in reporter creation/dedup, artificial issue #12 was closed after proof, and Required run 27847440478 passed on main after the ripgrep baseline-tool fix.

title: [W0-T3] Feature-profile + SemVer + stable-protocol lanes (semver-checks ADVISORY now)

WHY: Decision (b) commits us to a full supported-feature matrix + a blocking SemVer contract (from 0.3.0). This task stands up the machinery NOW, running ADVISORY so it does not block the W1-T3 API redesign; W2-T1 flips it to blocking.

SCOPE:
1. docs/SUPPORT.md supported profiles (minimal/default/all + each optional-integration combo: chrono/uuid/serde_json/rust_decimal/arrow/soda); exercise the FULL matrix with cargo hack.
2. Wire cargo-semver-checks — gate the LIBRARY crates oracledb-protocol + oracledb ONLY (it skips oracledb-derive, a proc-macro, and ERRORS if asked to check it); cover the derive's generated surface with a separate trybuild compile test.

4. Build+test oracledb-protocol on CURRENT STABLE so its stable-compatibility can't silently rot (it has zero asupersync refs + no nightly features; oracledb-protocol/src/lib.rs:1 forbids unsafe).

VERIFIED (R10): cargo-semver-checks 0.48.0 runs cleanly under nightly-2026-05-11 — check-release -p oracledb-protocol and -p oracledb both pass (196 checks each); does NOT choke on oracledb's transitive asupersync try_trait_v2 (its rustdoc-JSON step handles the nightly-only crate; ~60-75s cold build for oracledb). Proven non-trivial vs --baseline-rev 27fb6c3 (flags the ConnectOptions.statement_cache_size field-add as major-requiring). Offline runners pre-fetch the baseline + pass --baseline-rustdoc; the manual rustdoc-JSON fallback is NOT needed.

ACCEPTANCE: the full supported matrix compiles/tests; an unsupported combo is documented, not implied by --all-features; protocol crate green on stable; cargo-semver-checks verified runnable under the pin and wired ADVISORY.
TESTS: trybuild cases for the derive surface; a CI matrix job per profile; a semver-checks advisory run on each PR (reports, does not block, until W2-T1).
DEPS: W0-T1; pairs with W1 API work. Feeds W2-T1 (the gate flip) + W4-T1.

title: [W0-T4] Close the nto bead off-gate (ADR-0001 rationale; NOT a 1.0 gate)
description:
WHY: rust-oracledb-nto ("Replace PyO3 async thread offload with native async thin execution path") is the sole in_progress bead. The async-bridge investigation RESOLVED it as won't-fix: there is NO native PyO3↔asupersync bridge — asupersync 0.3.4 exposes no foreign-loop/external-waker surface and pyo3-async-runtimes supports only tokio/async-std. The pyshim's PyO3 experimental-async + spawn_blocking + per-op block_on is the CORRECT design (node-oracledb-analogous). The pyshim is publish=false, so this has ZERO release impact.

SCOPE: close rust-oracledb-nto with that rationale; record it as non-evidence / off the release DAG.
ACCEPTANCE: rust-oracledb-nto closed; recorded as non-gate PM cleanup.
TESTS: n/a (administrative).
DEPS: none (independent, off-gate). Related to existing bead rust-oracledb-nto.
close_reason: Completed: closed rust-oracledb-nto as off-gate/non-evidence per ADR-0001 async-bridge rationale; tests n/a for administrative cleanup.

title: [WAVE-1] Architecture — private seams + the permanent 1.0 public contract
description:
Wave 1 builds the private seams and the public API/contract that everything else rests on. Highest-stakes wave: the API designed here becomes a blocking SemVer contract at 0.3.0 (ADR-0002), so it must be deliberate.

Tasks: W1-T1 private transport/connector + ConnectionCore + scripted transport (unlocks cassette/DPOR/fault-matrix); W1-T2 session-recovery state machine (cancellation as a contract) incl. bounded recovery budget + checkpoint invariant; W1-T3 four-family operation API (query/execute/execute_many/register_query — see docs/API_DESIGN.md) incl. single op-deadline; W1-T4 calibrated type-evolution policy; W1-T5 ProtocolLimits; W1-T6 typed errors/disposition + Outcome/CancelKind discipline + redacted observability; W1-T7 async-native pool (sync facade); W1-T8 async↔blocking symmetry sweep; W1-T9 module/re-export coherence.

ALL of Wave 1 must land before the 0.3.0 migration (W2-T1). Architecture: async/blocking facades → operation reqs → private OperationCore → session recovery + typed errors/disposition + ProtocolLimits → private ConnectionCore → private WireTransport (TCP/TLS · Recording · Replay · Scripted). Pool: async pool core (acquire(&Cx).await · yielding waiter · region-owned reaper) → pure PoolState → effects, with a block_on blocking facade.
close_reason: WAVE 1 COMPLETE. All architecture tasks closed: W1-T1 (private transport/ConnectionCore), W1-T2 (session-recovery state machine + bounded recovery + checkpoints), W1-T3 (four-family API), W1-T4 (type-evolution policy), W1-T5 (ProtocolLimits), W1-T6 (typed errors/disposition + redacted observability), W1-T7 (async-native pool — reaper converted to async region task after adversarial verification caught the incomplete original + a real EDEADLK), W1-T8 (async/blocking symmetry), W1-T9 (module coherence + prelude), W1-T10 (pure PoolState). The private seams + permanent 1.0 public contract are in place.

title: [W1-T1] Private transport / connector + ConnectionCore + scripted transport
description:
WHY: One private seam beneath the public Connection lets PROD and ALL deterministic tests share the same framing/state — no drift, no permanent public test surface. The scripted transport is THE capability that unlocks cassette replay (W3-E6), DPOR (W3-E3/E4), and the fault matrix (W3-E5). Supersedes the v2 idea of public Mock variants / connect_over (resolves risks R6/R7).

SCOPE: introduce private WireTransport + Connector contracts beneath the public Connection; route production TCP/TLS, recording, strict replay, and a SCRIPTED transport through one ConnectionCore<T>. The scripted transport injects short read/write, Poll::Pending, EOF, errors, exact expected-writes, and virtual-time advances. Keep deterministic harnesses CRATE-LOCAL (they access private seams); do NOT publish a connect_over/Mock public surface. Make the accidental transport-half exports private during 0.3 (apply the W0-T5 ledger disposition: OracleReadHalf/OracleWriteHalf transport.rs:40/58).

ACCEPTANCE: a ConnectionCore runs connect/execute/fetch over a scripted transport, crate-local, zero sockets, no new public API.
TESTS: a crate-local test that drives connect→execute→fetch entirely over the scripted transport with zero sockets; assert exact expected-writes.
DEPS: W0-T5 (ledger dispositions). Foundational — feeds W1-T2, W1-T5, W1-T7, and W3-E3/E5/E6.
close_reason: Completed: W1-T1.1 through W1-T1.4 are closed. The driver now has private WireTransport/Connector seams, private transport halves, ConnectionCore<DriverTransport> routing production TCP/TLS plus recording/replay/scripted tests through shared framing/state, and crate-local scripted fault/virtual-time coverage with no new public API.

title: [W1-T10] Pure PoolState lifecycle model (deterministic, NO async deps — parallelizable)
description: WHY (Codex + self review, P2): the plan explicitly says the pure PoolState model 'has no deps and can land first', but as a subtask of W1-T7 it was gated behind W1-T2, losing that parallelism. Split out here as a STANDALONE, dependency-free task so a swarm can build + test it from day one. This is the precise oracle that shrinks the later DPOR model (W3-E4). SCOPE: the pure bookkeeping state machine — explicit states Opening/Idle/CheckedOut/Validating/Retiring/Closing/Closed; counts DERIVED from state (not tracked separately); effects(open/ping/close) modeled as EVENTS (no I/O, no sockets, no async); specified waiter ordering, acquire-cancel, reap, graceful-vs-force close. ACCEPTANCE: pure model with deterministic unit tests over the FULL lifecycle; counts derived; effects event-driven; zero async/sockets. TESTS: deterministic lifecycle unit tests with detailed step logging. DEPS: none (parallelizable from the start). Feeds W1-T7 (the async pool wires this behind an async mutex + yielding waiter queue) and W3-E4 (DPOR models this shell).
close_reason: Completed: added a private pool::lifecycle PurePoolState model with explicit Opening/Idle/CheckedOut/Validating/Retiring/Closing/Closed states, derived counts, ordered waiter queue, event-driven Open/Ping/Close effects, acquire cancellation, reap, and graceful-vs-force close behavior. The model is not wired into the current blocking PoolEngine yet; W1-T7 will integrate it behind the async pool. Verified locally: cargo test -p oracledb pool --lib passed 11 tests including 6 pure lifecycle tests; cargo test -p oracledb --lib passed 66 tests; cargo clippy -p oracledb --lib --no-deps -- -D warnings passed; cargo fmt --all -- --check passed; git diff --check passed.

title: [W1-T5-fup] ProtocolLimits: add oversized-length property tests + wire-or-remove the redirect_loops limit
description: WHY (adversarial verification of closed W1-T5, P2): W1-T5's acceptance demanded 'boundary∓1 tests in unit/property/fuzz' and 'no parser allocates from an unchecked wire length'. Two residual gaps were found: (1) PROPERTY tests for oversized-length rejection do not exist (only boundary unit tests) — add proptest cases over arbitrary oversized length prefixes asserting rejection BEFORE allocation, for every ProtocolLimits family; this also feeds W3-E2 fuzz. (2) the 'redirect_loops' limit is declared + unit-tested but wired to NOTHING (no redirect is actually followed/counted) — either wire it into the connect-redirect path so it bounds real redirect chains, or remove the dead limit and its test. ACCEPTANCE: property tests present + green; redirect_loops either gates a real code path (with a test that exceeding it errors) or is removed. DEPS: discovered-from W1-T5 (core ProtocolLimits already landed).
close_reason: ProtocolLimits property tests + redirect resolution. Added crates/oracledb-protocol/tests/protocol_limits_properties.rs (proptest, 8 properties over all 11 families): oversized length prefixes rejected with the typed ProtocolError::ResourceLimit BEFORE allocation, proven with the allocation-counter global allocator + real decoders (vector/response_bytes/lob_chunks/packet). redirect_loops/max_redirects REMOVED as dead: the connect path rejects on the FIRST redirect packet (Error::RedirectUnsupported, lib.rs:3501), never follows/loops — the limit bounded a chain that cannot form (zero production call sites, grep-confirmed). Independently verified: property tests pass in cargo test --workspace real-exit 0 x2; redirect removal + first-redirect-rejection read-confirmed.

title: [W1-T2] Session-recovery state machine (cancellation as a contract)
description:
WHY: The public contract must become "every completed op leaves the session reusable or dead." This is also the DPOR invariant (W3-E3) and exactly what oraclemcp needs. Supersedes the v2 "make drain public" note (drain stays private on both surfaces).

SCOPE:
1. An explicit internal machine: Ready → InFlight → BreakSent → Draining → Ready|Dead. Only Ready may start an op; each response belongs to exactly one op; drain is at-most-once; close is idempotent. BREAK/RESET/drain stay PRIVATE (do not expose drain_cancel_response). CancelHandle only REQUESTS cancellation; the op-completion path reconciles the wire and returns the session Ready or Dead.
2. BOUNDED RECOVERY BUDGET (pairs with the single op-deadline, W1-T3 / API_DESIGN.md principle 7): the BREAK→Draining cleanup runs under its OWN fresh, bounded recovery deadline, independent of the op's already-expired deadline — otherwise the very timeout/cancel that triggered recovery would instantly cancel the drain and force Dead on a recoverable session. Only a SECOND failure during the bounded drain yields Dead. MECHANISM NOTE (verified against asupersync 0.3.4): Cx::masked (cx/cx.rs:2238) masks only a SYNCHRONOUS finalize closure (mask_depth/MaskGuard, invariant inv.cancel.mask_bounded), so the ASYNC drain is protected by a fresh recovery sub-context/deadline, NOT by wrapping the await in masked. (Today recovery re-arms a fresh relative timeout_ms — recover_from_call_timeout(cx, timeout_ms), lib.rs:1926; the single-deadline move makes the explicit bounded recovery budget NECESSARY, not optional.)
3. CANCELLATION-OBSERVABILITY INVARIANT: every multi-round-trip loop (fetch-batch continuation, LOB chunk, recovery drain, retry) carries a cx.checkpoint() so a pending cancel is observed BETWEEN round-trips. Checkpoints already exist pervasively (~20+ sites incl. the fetch region lib.rs:3081/3134/3195/3229); this CODIFIES it as a contract that the W3-E3 DPOR cancel paths verify — it is NOT "add missing checkpoints."

ACCEPTANCE: the machine is the single owner of recovery; property/DPOR tests assert the ready-or-dead invariant across success/timeout/cancel/error/dropped-future; a recoverable post-timeout drain yields Ready (not Dead).
TESTS: property + DPOR (W3-E3) over the five wire/cancel paths; a unit test that a second-timeout-during-drain yields Dead while a recoverable drain yields Ready.
DEPS: W1-T1. Feeds W1-T3 (deadline), W1-T6 (disposition), W1-T7 (return-to-pool), W1-T8 (cancel symmetry), W3-E3.
close_reason: Completed: W1-T2.1 through W1-T2.4 are closed with the private SessionRecoveryPhase owner for Ready/InFlight/BreakSent/Draining/Dead transitions, bounded no-ambient recovery after expired caller contexts, codified cancellation checkpoints between round trips, and request-only async CancelHandle cancellation with owner-side Ready/Dead reconciliation and duplicate BREAK suppression. Child work passed focused tests plus workspace fmt, clippy, tests, diff check, and dependency-cycle gates.

Today the code has Connection::cancel(&Cx) (async, lib.rs:4361) and a SYNC CancelHandle::cancel(&mut self) (lib.rs:4933) — but NO async handle API. This bead owns creating it. SCOPE: add an async CancelHandle::cancel(&Cx) that REQUESTS cancellation of the in-flight op via the W1-T2 state machine (request-only; the op-completion path reconciles the wire and returns Ready|Dead). Keep the existing sync wrapper for the blocking facade. ACCEPTANCE: an async CancelHandle::cancel(&Cx) exists and is exercised by W3-E3 P3. TESTS: a unit test that cancel(&Cx) transitions Ready->BreakSent and the op reconciles to Ready or Dead. DEPS: W1-T2.1 (the state machine). Feeds W3-E3 (P3) + W1-T8 (cancel symmetry).

title: [W1-T4] Type-evolution policy (calibrated, not blanket #[non_exhaustive])
description:
WHY: Blanket non_exhaustive makes additions POSSIBLE but imposes immediate caller costs; accessor/builder APIs are more usable for 1.x. Calibrated to effort (oraclemcp is the consumer): accessor-ize the genuinely-evolvable types; don't gold-plate fixed ones. This is what makes the blocking SemVer contract (ADR-0002) survivable — future Oracle types/fields land additively.

SCOPE (by type class, during 0.3):
- options/config (ConnectOptions, ExecuteOptions-derived request types, PoolConfig, ArrowFetchOptions): private fields + builders + getters.
- results/metadata (Rows/ExecuteOutcome/BatchOutcome, ColumnMetadata, DecodedObject, DbmsOutput, BatchServerError): accessor APIs so representation can change without caller destructuring.
- open enums (Error :679, SessionlessError :890, PoolError, SodaError, ConversionError, ArrowConversionError, NotificationOutcome, ProtocolError, SqlError, wallet/DN errors): selective #[non_exhaustive].
- value enums (BindValue types.rs:444, QueryValue/QueryValueRef): add stable as_*/conversion/visitor APIs BEFORE #[non_exhaustive] so callers aren't forced into fragile variant matches.
- fixed wire-image structs: keep internal or document as fixed.

ACCEPTANCE: an OUT-OF-WORKSPACE fixture proves construct/match/additive-evolution; adding a field/variant is non-breaking for supported types. (The pyshim is in-workspace so unaffected; the additivity scratch test MUST be out-of-workspace to be meaningful.)
TESTS: the out-of-workspace fixture crate that constructs/matches the types and still compiles after a simulated field/variant addition.
DEPS: W0-T5 (ledger), W1-T3 (the types). Feeds W2-T1.
close_reason: Type-evolution policy complete across all children: W1-T4.1 (options/config private fields+builders), W1-T4.2 (results/metadata accessors), W1-T4.3 (open-enum #[non_exhaustive]), W1-T4.4 (value-enum accessors-before-non_exhaustive), W1-T4.5 (out-of-workspace additivity fixture, independently built green). Adding a field/variant to a supported type is non-breaking — verified, not asserted.

title: [W1-T5] ProtocolLimits — centralized resource bounds + checked allocation
description:
WHY: An MCP server consumes arbitrary server responses. BoundedReader closed the per-read OOM class but NOT the cumulative size / object-depth / redirect-loop / repeated-frame classes. One policy, used everywhere, makes the driver resource-bounded by construction (a North-Star property).

SCOPE: one ProtocolLimits policy used by every decoder/state machine — packet/frame size, cumulative response bytes, columns, binds, batch rows, object depth/elements, vector dims, LOB chunks, redirect loops, and every length-prefixed collection. Validate + checked-arith BEFORE allocate/index. Typed ResourceLimit error (limit name + observed value) with a defined connection disposition (some pre-sync → Ready; some → Dead).

ACCEPTANCE: boundary∓1 tests in unit/property/fuzz; malformed-input stress stays within documented memory/time ceilings; NO parser allocates from an unchecked wire length.
TESTS: boundary∓1 unit tests per limit; property tests that random oversized lengths are rejected pre-allocation; fuzz targets (W3-E2) stay within RSS ceilings.
DEPS: W1-T1. Feeds W3-E2 (fuzz manifest references the limits).
close_reason: Completed: W1-T5.1 added the ProtocolLimits policy, W1-T5.2 wired the policy through packet/reassembly and decoder/state-machine paths with checked allocation/indexing, and W1-T5.3 added typed ResourceLimit extraction plus pre-sync Ready vs post-sync Dead disposition. Boundary and regression tests cover every resource family; malformed oversized inputs fail before unchecked allocation; gates passed across the wave including cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --workspace --no-deps -- -D warnings, cargo test --workspace, git diff --check, and br dep cycles.

Gates passed: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --workspace --no-deps -- -D warnings, cargo test -p oracledb-protocol --lib, cargo test -p oracledb --lib, cargo test --workspace, git diff --check, br dep cycles.

title: [W1-T6] Structured errors, recovery hints, Outcome/CancelKind discipline, redacted observability
description:
WHY: Promoted from optional differentiator to FOUNDATIONAL — it is exactly what oraclemcp needs and pairs with W1-T2. Callers must classify + decide disposition via METHODS, never by parsing display strings.

SCOPE:
1. Stable inspection: Error::kind()->ErrorKind (network/timeout/cancel/protocol/database/conversion/pool/resource-limit), oracle_code(), connection_disposition()->{Reusable,Dead}, conservative retry_hint() (never auto-retry non-idempotent ops). Prevalidate bind shape/type where unambiguous.
2. INTERNAL Outcome/CancelKind DISCIPLINE (verified asupersync 0.3.4): keep the four-valued Outcome<T,E> (types/outcome.rs:216) and CancelKind/CancelReason (types/cancel.rs:264/521) threaded internally and branch on the cancel kind — Timeout (drain + set retry_hint where idempotent), Shutdown (close the connection), RaceLost (loser drains quietly) — to drive connection_disposition(); flatten Outcome→Result ONLY at the public boundary, mapping Cancelled to a distinct cancel/timeout error variant (never a generic I/O error).
3. Opt-in tracing/pool metrics record phase, duration, statement fingerprint, rows/bytes, pool wait, cancel phase, disposition — RAW SQL/binds/credentials EXCLUDED by default, covered by redaction tests.

ACCEPTANCE: classification + disposition via methods (no display-string parsing); redaction tests prove no secret leakage.
TESTS: unit tests mapping representative ORA errors to kind/disposition/retry_hint; redaction tests asserting SQL/binds/credentials never appear in emitted spans/metrics by default.
DEPS: W1-T2. Feeds W2-T1 + W3-E7 (typed-errors contract).
close_reason: All children complete: W1-T6.1 (error classification, closed earlier), W1-T6.2 (Outcome/CancelKind disposition discipline, wired + tested), W1-T6.3 (redacted observability, tested). Structured errors + recovery hints + disposition + redacted tracing — the foundational diagnosability W1-T6 specified.

title: [W1-T6.1] Error::kind/oracle_code/connection_disposition/retry_hint + prevalidation
description: Stable inspection methods; conservative retry_hint (never non-idempotent); prevalidate bind shape/type where unambiguous. ACCEPTANCE: classification via methods, no display-string parsing.
close_reason: Completed

title: [W1-T6.2] Outcome/CancelKind internal discipline
description: Branch on Timeout/Shutdown/RaceLost internally to drive disposition; flatten Outcome→Result only at the boundary; Cancelled→distinct error variant. ACCEPTANCE: a Shutdown-cancel closes the conn; a Timeout-cancel drains + may set retry_hint.
close_reason: CancelKind->CancelDisposition mapping (lib.rs:653-682: Timeout/Deadline/PollQuota/CostBudget->Timeout drain+reusable; Shutdown/ResourceUnavailable/LinkedExit->Close; User/RaceLost/FailFast/ParentCancelled->Cancel drain quietly) WIRED into the op-completion/recovery path (lib.rs:6821-6829: a Close disposition calls recovery.mark_dead()+self.dead=true; others stay reusable), and flattened to DISTINCT public Error variants at the boundary (into_error: CallTimeout / Cancelled[ORA-01013] — never a generic Runtime/Io), classifying via the W1-T6.1 methods. Tests: cancel_kind_maps_to_disposition (exhaustive per-kind), cancel_disposition_flattens_to_distinct_error_variants (asserts kind/oracle_code/connection_disposition/retry_hint, and NOT Runtime/Io). Independently verified: fmt/clippy/test real-exit 0; integration read-confirmed.

title: [W1-T6.3] Opt-in tracing/pool metrics + redaction tests
description: Phase/duration/fingerprint/rows/bytes/pool-wait/cancel-phase/disposition; raw SQL/binds/credentials excluded by default. ACCEPTANCE: redaction tests prove no secret leakage; default build pulls in nothing.

Redaction tests under --features tracing: spans_redact_sql_binds_and_credentials_by_default (captures emitted spans; asserts no password/PAN/SQL-literal appears in ANY field value or name; non-vacuous guard + positive control that the parameterized digest DOES survive) and sql_digest_redacts_embedded_string_and_numeric_literals. Independently verified: cargo test -p oracledb --features tracing real-exit 0; test bodies read-confirmed non-vacuous.

title: [W1-T7] Async-native connection pool (sync facade) — loom dropped, DPOR covers it
description:
DECISION (binding): the pool is ASYNC-NATIVE; sync is a block_on facade. Today pool.rs is SYNCHRONOUS — Mutex<PoolState> + Condvar waiters + a blocking reaper std::thread, with acquire a sync fn (pool.rs:234) returning a u64 handle from a low-level PoolEngine (zero async fn). That Condvar/"no foreign locks held" design exists ONLY so the PyO3 pyshim can release the GIL before blocking. The pyshim is publish=false and MUST NOT dictate the shipped driver's architecture. Every other part of the driver is "async core + thin block_on sync facade" (Connection/BlockingConnection); the pool is the lone inversion, which forces an async consumer (e.g. oraclemcp on asupersync) to BLOCK a runtime worker on a Condvar — the canonical "blocking call inside a cooperative runtime" anti-pattern. (There is no GIL in Rust; the GIL constraint was never the driver's — only the test shim's.)

SCOPE (8 points):
1. Async core: acquire(&self, cx:&Cx, opts:AcquireOptions) -> Result<PooledConnection,PoolError>. Fast path takes an idle conn; slow path registers in an ORDERED waiter queue and .awaits a YIELDING wakeup (asupersync Notify/semaphore — NO Condvar, NO parked OS thread). Acquire timeout = a bounded deadline/Budget on the wait; cx.checkpoint() rides the wait.
2. Cancel-safety (a DPOR invariant): if the acquire future is dropped in the hand-off race window, the waiter removes itself and any granted connection is RETURNED to the pool — never leaked, never double-handed.
3. Sync facade = block_on(acquire), exposed like BlockingConnection mirrors Connection (a Blocking* surface, NOT an _blocking suffix); final naming per W1-T8. Mirror close/drain/stats/release.
4. Region-owned reaper: close(&Cx).await cancels+joins it deterministically; a bare drop lets the owning region ABORT it (no await in Drop — R11), so it never leaks. Runs open/ping/close as async effects OUTSIDE the state lock, fed back as events.
5. Pure PoolState retained: states Opening/Idle/CheckedOut/Validating/Retiring/Closing/Closed; counts DERIVED from state; effects as events; specified waiter ordering / acquire-cancel / reap / graceful-vs-force close. Now behind an async mutex + yielding queue; concurrency explored by DPOR (W3-E4), not loom.
6. PooledConnection guard + the Drop problem: async checkin CANNOT run in Drop, so drop ENQUEUES a non-blocking "return" event reconciled by the next acquire / the reaper, with a bounded sync fallback; Drop never blocks or spawns (R11). An explicit release(&Cx).await is offered. Returned conns reconciled to Ready (W1-T2) or retired.
7. Visibility: PoolEngine<B> (W0-T5 leak candidate, pool.rs:164) → pub(crate); public surface is the async Pool + its sync facade.
8. pyshim keeps driving the BLOCKING facade; its GIL handling stays the pyshim's concern (it already does spawn_blocking). No Condvar/GIL rationale survives in the shipped crate.

ACCEPTANCE: async acquire YIELDS under contention (no OS thread parked — observable in the DPOR/lab schedule); a cancel mid-acquire leaks/duplicates no connection; the sync facade is exactly block_on of the async path; the reaper is joined on close(&Cx).await and aborted (not leaked) on bare drop; the pure PoolState has full-lifecycle deterministic tests with derived counts; harness/run.sh diff stays green.
TESTS: deterministic unit tests over the pure PoolState lifecycle; W3-E4 DPOR for the concurrency invariants (no double-handout/lost-wakeup/leaked-waiter/returned-retired/idempotent-close/acquire-cancel-returns).
DEPS: W1-T1 (mint/ping Connections via ConnectionCore), W1-T2 (return-to-pool disposition). The pure PoolState sub-model has no deps and can land first. Feeds W3-E4, and (via W1-T8) the pool symmetry.
close_reason: Async-native pool complete: reaper conversion landed (W1-T7.4). W1-T7 acceptance now met — async acquire yields (no parked thread), the reaper is an async region task (no Condvar/std::thread in the production path), close(&Cx).await cooperatively async-joins it (no executor block), drop aborts without leaking. Independently verified + adversarially read.

The pure PoolState now proves cancel after CheckedOut hands the slot to the next waiter, and runtime pool tests prove both dropping a registered pending acquire and dropping after a returned connection is handed to the request de-register the waiter and leave the connection reusable with no duplicate busy entry. title: [W1-T7.3] Sync facade = block_on (Blocking* pattern)

W1-T7.4/W1-T7.6 remain for async reaper and guard/drop surface work.

title: [W1-T7.4] Region-owned reaper (close joins; drop aborts)
description: Reaper is an async task owned by the pool region; close(&Cx).await joins it; bare drop aborts it (no await in Drop); ping/open/close run as effects OUTSIDE the lock. ACCEPTANCE: no leaked task on close OR drop.
close_reason: Reaper converted to a region-owned ASYNC task on a pool-owned dedicated asupersync Runtime (Condvar->Notify; cooperative reaper_stop+wake; cx.checkpoint per loop; all create/ping/close I/O OFF the state lock). close(&Cx).await async-joins the reaper TaskJoinHandle (NO std-thread join); bare drop requests shutdown without awaiting (R11). A real EDEADLK self-join deadlock found during empirical risk-proving was fixed by owning the Runtime on the EXTERNAL PoolEngine handle (reaper holds only Weak<EngineInner>+RuntimeHandle; last-handle via Arc::strong_count(&runtime); PoolEngine::drop closes conns then drops the runtime from OUTSIDE the worker thread). 4 risk integration tests (tests/pool_reaper_async.rs) + the in-crate cooperative-join test pass deterministically. Independently re-verified: fmt/clippy clean, full workspace suite green (no failures), risk suite 4/4 x5, pool suite 28/28 x3, production path grep-clean of Condvar/std::thread, and an adversarial code read confirming no lost wakeup / no self-join / cooperative cross-runtime join.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: REOPENED by adversarial verification (2 confirmed P1). The reaper was NOT converted to async: pool.rs:760 still declares bg:Condvar; pool.rs:1461 spawns it via std::thread::Builder::spawn(bg_main); the worker parks the OS thread at pool.rs:2326/2330 (bg.wait_timeout/bg.wait). All open/ping/idle-sweep/close run in that OS thread. W1-T7 item 4 (ROAD_TO_1_0.md:398-402) requires an ASYNC region-owned task (no std::thread, no Condvar), close(&Cx).await cancels+joins it, drop aborts it. ALSO: close_async (pool.rs:1605->close_impl 1610->handle.join() 1620) blocks the async executor on a synchronous OS-thread join — the runtime-blocking anti-pattern this bead set out to remove. The async ACQUIRE path is fine (yields on Notify); Drop is fine (no join). FIX: make the reaper an asupersync region task; async-join on close; abort on drop.

Verified locally: cargo fmt --all --check; cargo test -p oracledb pool::tests --lib; cargo test -p oracledb --lib; cargo check -p oracledb; cargo check -p oracledb-pyshim; scripts/check_api_ledger.sh; scripts/gen_baseline.sh; cargo test --workspace; cargo clippy --workspace --no-deps -- -D warnings; git diff --check; br dep cycles --json.

title: [W1-T8] async↔blocking symmetry sweep (incl. the pool)
description:
WHY: Asymmetry is a silent papercut and contradicts W1-T3's "BlockingConnection mirrors 1:1." This task VERIFIES that claim across the whole surface (connections AND the W1-T7 pool) and standardizes naming.

SCOPE: make the two public surfaces mirror exactly. VERIFIED genuine I/O gaps to fill: BlockingConnection::cancel is missing (async :4361; only CancelHandle::cancel :4933 exists); CQN recv_notification/notify_register (:1816/1787) are async-only; free_temp_lobs (:3910) and trim_lob (:3873) have only *_with_timeout blocking twins; execute_query_with_bind_rows_and_options (:2711, no-timeout) is async-only. DELIBERATELY async-only (document, don't wrap): the zero-copy _ref fetch family + direct-path (borrow lifetimes don't cross block_on). The v2 "make drain_cancel_response public" gap is MOOTED by W1-T2 (drain stays private). Standardize naming x / x_with_timeout / x_named. POOL SURFACE (W1-T7): the async Pool + its blocking facade are part of this sweep — acquire/close/drain/stats/release mirror 1:1 (or carry a documented async-only exception), named per the BlockingConnection pattern (a Blocking* surface, not an _blocking suffix); this is where the W1-T7 facade naming is finalized.

ACCEPTANCE: a GENERATED table shows 1:1 async↔blocking coverage or an explicit documented exception per method (connections + pool).
TESTS: a generated coverage table checked in CI (fails if a new async method lacks a blocking twin or documented exception).
DEPS: W1-T3 (final method set), W1-T2 (cancel semantics), W1-T7 (the pool's two surfaces).

title: [WAVE-2] Migration release 0.3.0 — ship API, migrate consumers, flip the SemVer gate to blocking
description:
Wave 2 is the hinge between architecture and qualification. Single task W2-T1: publish 0.3.0 carrying the Wave-1 API (old execute names kept as #[deprecated] shims), migrate the first-party consumers (the ~17 pyshim execute call-sites + oraclemcp) to the four operation families, then FLIP cargo-semver-checks from advisory to BLOCKING with the published 0.3.0 public API snapshot as the baseline.

Why a dedicated migration release (ADR-0002): give the real downstream one published release to exercise the new API; do the breaking cleanup HERE, not at/after 1.0. From 0.3.1 onward an unintended break fails CI; an intentional break takes a minor bump (0.4.0) + a baseline refresh. Deprecation shims are removed before 1.0.0-rc.1 (W4-T1). Anti-drift guard throughout: harness/run.sh diff stays green.
close_reason: WAVE-2 COMPLETE: 0.3.0 migration release published (four families + execute_raw + deprecations), first-party pyshim migrated (oraclemcp delegated), SemVer gate flipped to blocking, AND all 103 differential-conformance gaps closed (0 regressions, 2578/2578).

title: [W2-T1] Publish 0.3.0 + migrate first-party consumers + flip SemVer gate to BLOCKING
description:
WHY (ADR-0002): give the real downstream ONE published release to exercise the new four-family API; do the breaking cleanup HERE, not at/after 1.0. This is the hinge: when 0.3.0 ships, its public API becomes the cargo-semver-checks BASELINE and the gate flips from advisory to BLOCKING — from 0.3.1 onward an unintended break fails CI.

SCOPE:
1. Ship the W1 API/architecture with old execute names as #[deprecated] shims (removed before RC, W4-T1).
2. Migrate the ~17 pyshim execute call-sites (conn.rs:142,184,333,350,368,398; cursor.rs:1077,1411,1420,1446,1643; async_conn.rs:136; async_cursor.rs:74,85,117,201; pool.rs:126; subscr.rs:336) and oraclemcp to the four operation families — hand edits, file-by-file per AGENTS.md.
3. Publish 0.3.0 (mind the inter-crate version pins — oracledb/Cargo.toml:71-72 are hardcoded and NOT bumped by the workspace version; this bit us at 0.2.1/0.2.2; W4-T3 adds the preflight guard).
4. FLIP THE GATE: snapshot the published public API (per supported profile) as the cargo-semver-checks baseline and flip from advisory to BLOCKING in required CI. From 0.3.1 onward an unintended break fails CI; an intentional break takes a minor bump (0.4.0) + refreshes the baseline in the same release.

ACCEPTANCE: 0.3.0 published; pyshim + oraclemcp on the new API; conformance suite (harness/run.sh diff) green; deprecations scheduled for removal pre-RC; semver-checks baseline committed and the gate is BLOCKING on main.
TESTS: full conformance suite green post-migration; a CI run proving the blocking gate now fails on a deliberately-introduced break.
DEPS: ALL of Wave 1 (W1-T1..T9). Feeds the entire Wave 3 (E1/E2/E5/E6/E7/E9) + indirectly E8/W4.

title: [WAVE-3] Qualification — exact-SHA bounded evidence beyond the python-oracledb suite
description:
Wave 3 builds the correctness arsenal that pushes confidence BEYOND python-oracledb's own thin test suite, then certifies it. Evidence rules: fixed seeds in required CI + rotating seeds in canary/soak; record tool versions, bounds (CPU/time/state), corpus hashes, target manifests; severity triage (no open P0/P1, no untriaged finding, P2 needs a fix or a tracked technical exception with rationale); call a model \"exhaustive\" ONLY when its finite queue empties under recorded bounds, else \"bounded clean.\"

Tasks: E1 FromSql/ToSql property round-trips; E2 manifest-driven fuzzing (+9 targets); E3 DPOR over wire/cancel paths; E4 DPOR over the async pool (loom dropped); E5 deterministic fault/fragmentation matrix; E6 secure full-connection cassette replay; E7 live support matrix + direct oraclemcp contract suite; E8 multi-pass bug-hunt sweeps; E9 perf/resource regression gates; E10 post-1.0 idea-wizard backlog (demoted).

CRITICAL: Wave-3 development runs are DISCOVERY (on moving commits). The single QUALIFYING run that must be green on one exact SHA is W4-T2 on the frozen RC (ADR-0003). Wave 3 builds the suites; W4-T2 certifies them together.
